comments: Potential does not need suffering..it needs discipline through discomfort.It is about staying consistent when it is uncomfortable.Potential without effort is just imagination.
Potential feels powerful until effort is required. People love the idea, not the process it demands. Growth only pays those willing to stay uncomfortable.

Potential doesn’t disappear because of difficulty Raj, it gets blocked by how we respond to it. If every challenge feels overwhelming, the nervous system starts associating growth with stress. And that’s when people unconsciously hold themselves back. The real work is building the capacity to stay steady while you evolve.

A stark reminder that talent is merely a multiplier, while effort is the base. In leadership, the goal isn't just to identify high-potential individuals, but to cultivate a culture where the "suffering" of skill-building is viewed as a necessary investment in excellence. Potential only becomes an asset when it is forged through consistent, disciplined action.

True. Potential feels powerful in theory, but in reality it demands discomfort, consistency, and patience. Most people don’t lack talent, they quit during the boring, repetitive phase of building it. That’s where the real gap is created.
Potential is cheap. Execution is expensive.Most people admire the outcome.Few commit to the process.Growth isn’t talent — it’s tolerance for discomfort.The real edge? Staying when it gets hard.
Potential only has value when it’s acted upon. Growth isn’t comfortable—it demands effort, discipline, and showing up even when it’s hard.
Potential means nothing without the reps.Growth isn’t pretty, but that’s where the real change happens.

Potential is basically just the participation trophy of adulthood until you actually do the work. We all love the 'Main Character' energy, but nobody wants to film the training montage where they actually fail a few times. If it doesn't hurt a little, you're probably just daydreaming in high definition."बिन तपस्या न मिले फल, कोरी बातों में क्या दम है।क्षमता धूल समान है तेरी, जो पसीने से न नम है।

Potential is just a theoretical ceiling. Action and discipline are the floor. Most people are in love with the 'idea' of being great but not the reality of getting there. The suffering is usually just the cost of admission for growth.

Potential without the work behind it is just a story you tell yourself. everyone has things they could be good at, but that gap between could be and actually is only closes through the part that isn't comfortable or exciting. the suffering isn't separate from the development. it's exactly how the development happens.

A stack reminder that talent is merely a multiplier while effort is the base in leadership the goal isn't just to identify high potential individuals but to cultivate a culture where the sufferring of skill building is viewed as a necessary investment in excellence potential only becomes an asset when it is forged through consistent disciplined action !!!

hook_text_english: India is 5th largest economy yet has world's highest child malnutrition rate.
full_text_english: India is the world's 5th largest economy. It is also home to the highest rate of child malnutrition in the world.In our recent episode, I talked to Ravinder Grover, COO of HarvestPlus Solutions, about why these two facts exist in the same country at the same time.He explained that nutrition, like zinc and iron, has been disappearing from our food, which our bodies need every day. Scientists call it 'hidden hunger’, which means that the food you eat tastes the same as before, and you feel full, but the inside of your body is starving because the nutrition is just not there anymore.It started a few decades back when India needed to grow a lot more food to feed its people. So the seeds were changed to grow as much as possible. And it worked. But in that process, we also lost the nutrients.Today, we have to eat the double of what our grandparents did to get the same nutrition.The zinc in our crops has dropped by 30% in the last 50 years. And the consequences have been building ever since.67% of Indian children have anaemia. 57% of Indian women have anaemia. Most of them don’t even have an idea.And it all started with a need for optimisation that nobody thought to question.Full episode out now, watch it here: https://lnkd.in/d6anGApmhashtag#rajshamani hashtag#figuringout
body_summary_english: Episode explores hidden hunger—nutritional deficiency in food that looks and tastes normal but leaves bodies starving. Guest COO explains why nutrition has disappeared from food despite economic growth.

One thing that we must understand is that we got an immense potential and a unique ability to put forward. But the main question is, do you know your unique skills? Find that out and work harder, you will definitely reach there.
Psychologically, this is post-traumatic growth in action. Difficulty builds neural pathways for resilience. Discomfort is literally how the brain rewires for success.
Developing potential is less about talent and more about tolerance and how much uncertainty, failure, and repetition you can handle.
Growth, if you really embrace it, is painful. But the security you'll have about your ability to endure anything...That’s an amazing gift to have in life. You'll learn antifragility.
Being the 5th largest economy means nothing if our people aren't nourished enough to participate in it this is the real infrastructure gap we need to solve.

Absolutely true. Potential is like a seed; it remains just a possibility until it endures the darkness and pressure of the soil to break open and grow. Growth is almost always born in the womb of discomfort. Brilliant insight

This is a powerful reminder that solving one problem at scale can quietly create another.We increased food production, but overlooked nutrition. The result is a system where people feel full, yet remain deficient.The next shift isn’t just about quantity, it’s about quality.

We didn’t just optimize agriculture—we optimized nutrients out of it. The real crisis isn’t food scarcity, it’s quality blindness; we measure yield per acre but ignore health per bite. If growth creates hidden deficits, is it progress—or just delayed damage?

hook_text_english: Is food today as nutritious as it used to be?
full_text_english: Comment ‘Link’ and I’ll personally DM you the full conversation.Do you think the food you eat today is as nutritious as it used to be?In today’s episode, we sit down with @ravindergrover, COO - @hps_scaleimpact, to talk about nutrition, hidden hunger, and why food today is not as healthy as we think.Here’s what we discussed:-Hidden Hunger Explained: Why millions are undernourished despite eating enough.-Nutrition Loss in Food: How staples like roti have lost essential nutrients over time.-Protein & Deficiency Crisis: Why more people are talking about protein today.-Biofortification & Global Solutions: How countries like Kenya tackled iron deficiency.-India’s Nutrition Gap: Policy challenges, ignored science, and what needs to change.Catch the full episode here:YouTube Channel: Raj ShamaniSpotify Channel: Raj Shamani Figuring OutUntil then, keep figuring out! 🚀#rajshamani #figuringout

comments: Important reminder—talent is often overrated, consistency isn’t. Most people quit before they get good. Skill is built through repetition, feedback, and patience. The gap between average and exceptional is usually just practice sustained over time. If you stay consistent, what feels difficult today eventually becomes your natural strength.
Sarita Dhar Dubey Talent sets the baseline → consistency sets the ceiling. The 'boring' daily reps that everyone else avoids are exactly what manufacture an "overnight" success.
Talent is often just practice people witnessed late. Most quit in the phase where repetition looks unimpressive. Mastery begins where insecurity stops you.
Sagar Kapoor Mastery is simply surviving the phase where you still look like an amateur. We usually quit when our ego overrides our commitment to the process.

The psychology underneath this is worth unpacking. What feels like lack of talent is almost always lack of reps combined with comparison to someone else’s visible output rather than their invisible hours. The brain is remarkably good at attributing other people’s skill to innate ability and our own gaps to fundamental inadequacy. It’s called the fundamental attribution error applied inward. Practice doesn’t just build skill. It builds the tolerance for being bad at something long enough to get good at it. That tolerance is actually the harder thing to develop than the skill itself.

Rajdeep Bodh Ego suspension is exactly the right framing Rajdeep. The willingness to look incompetent in the short term is what separates people who develop mastery from people who stay comfortable. And it is harder for high achievers than most because they have the most identity invested in already being good. The gap between romanticising the outcome and tolerating the process is where most people quietly give up without ever naming it as giving up.

Hard truth. Often we give up using Lack of Talent as an excuse, when the real gap is actually the Lack of Repetitions. Raj, do you think it is possible to avoid burnout during this practice phase, or is it just an inevitable part of the process?

Gopal Sharma Great question, Gopal. Burnout usually doesn't come from the volume of work → it comes from working without a clear feedback loop. Fatigue is inevitable, but burnout is mitigated when you build strict recovery systems directly into your practice framework.

Sometimes it’s not just about practicing more…it’s about practicing the right way.A lot of people repeat the same thing for years and don’t improve.Progress comes when you get feedback, adjust, and stay consistent.
D Ajay Kumar Exactly, Ajay. Blind repetition = compounding flaws. Deliberate practice requires execution → feedback → micro-adjustments. Volume without direction is just wasted stamina.

This hits differently once you understand how the brain actually works. It’s not talent, it’s repetition shaping neural pathways. The real challenge isn’t passion, it’s staying consistent when the brain resists discomfort.
True. But how do you differentiate between lack of practice vs wrong practice? I feel many people stay stuck because they are repeating the same mistake.

Palak Maheshwari Palak, the separator is a strict feedback loop. Lack of practice feels like slow progress; wrong practice feels like hitting a brick wall. If 50 reps yield no evolution, the method needs an audit, not just more effort.

Consistency builds skill, but direction builds outcomes.In markets, repetition without structured feedback just compounds mistakes.Real edge comes from data-backed practice and systems that evolve with it.That’s where passion actually converts into performance.
Happening Market Official . Effort is the engine → Feedback is the steering wheel. Compounding mistakes at a high speed only gets you to the wrong destination faster.

Raj Shamani What stands out here is the gap between consuming ideas and compounding them through action. In my experience, most people don’t fail due to lack of information, they stall because they don’t convert clarity into consistent execution. The real edge is not knowing more, it’s applying faster and staying with it longer than others are willing to.

True. Most people don’t fail at their passion, they quit during the awkward phase when taste is high and skill is still catching up. Passion without repetition is just sentiment. Practice is what turns identity into competence.
True.But the bigger problem is people quit right before compounding starts. 3 months feels like “no results”3 years looks like “overnight success”

Raj Shamani Harsh truth:Most people don’t fail because they’re bad…They fail because they quit before they get good.Consistency beats passion. Every time.Do it daily.Do it badly.Do it anyway.That’s how “talent” is built. 🔥

hook_text_english: Your brain slows down at 2 pm—here's why and how to fix it.
full_text_english: You know how you can sit down at your desk in the morning and just get through things. Be it emails, decisions, or work in general, it feels manageable. But then, as soon as 2 pm arrives, things slow down. Everything feels twice as hard and half as fast.There is a well-documented reason why your brain does this every single afternoon, and there are simple ways to work around it.Check the doc below to know why this happens and how to fix it. hashtag#rajshamani hashtag#figuringout
body_summary_english: Explores the scientific reason for afternoon energy dips and provides simple solutions to work around the productivity decline that happens every afternoon.

Raj Shamani What stands out here is the gap between consuming ideas and compounding them through action. In my experience, most people don’t fail due to lack of information, they stall because they don’t convert clarity into consistent execution. The real edge is not knowing more, it’s applying faster and staying with it longer than others are willing to.

True. Most people don’t fail at their passion, they quit during the awkward phase when taste is high and skill is still catching up. Passion without repetition is just sentiment. Practice is what turns identity into competence.

Raj Shamani Harsh truth:Most people don’t fail because they’re bad…They fail because they quit before they get good.Consistency beats passion. Every time.Do it daily.Do it badly.Do it anyway.That’s how “talent” is built. 🔥
Practice without overthinking, while enjoying the process and staying undistracted, is what's missing today.And those who do it consistently? They’re the ones people call “talented.”

We often confuse 'lack of talent' with 'lack of volume.' The difference between a beginner and an expert is almost always just the amount of deliberate practice behind the scenes. Thanks for the reminder!
True  -  we often question the path when the real gap is in practice. What feels hard today becomes natural with repetition.
This changed my perspective Raj Shamani I used to think I wasn’t good enough, but now I see it’s just about showing up more often and practicing with intent.
You'll be shocked at how much practice is required to be really good at something. Think in terms of years not months or weeks.

True for skill. But for many founders, the barrier isn’t just practice.You can have a strong, disruptive idea with clear demand, and still struggle to move it forward. Not because of lack of effort, but because access to the right people, early believers, and experienced operators is limited.Practice sharpens ability. But without the right environment and network, even high potential ideas find it hard to take off.Execution needs repetition. Opportunity often needs access.

Consistency builds skill but direction builds outcomes in markets repetition without structured feedback just compounds mistakes real edge comes from data backed practice and systems that evolve with it that's where passion actually converts into performance

Very true ✨Our passion is never the problem—it’s the consistency we sometimes lack. When we keep practicing, that raw passion gradually takes shape into something truly beautiful.Practice doesn’t just polish our skills, it builds the confidence to finally bring our passion onto the stage and share it with the world 🌍✨Because in the end, passion without practice remains a dream—but with consistent effort, it becomes a story worth sharing 💫
Even passionate people lose direction by comparing themselves to success stories. But success isn’t overnight—practice builds mastery. Passion isn’t enough; mastery is what truly matters.

Yeh line thodi motivating lagti hai, lekin har case mein true nahi hoti. Kabhi-kabhi problem practice ki nahi, clarity ki hoti hai. Log saalon tak practice karte rehte hain, par direction galat hoti hai to growth ruk jaati hai.Mere hisaab se better version yeh hota: right practice + feedback + consistency. Sirf “more practice” bolna thoda oversimplification hai.Aur ek aur cheez, har passion skill banne ke liye nahi hota. Kuch cheezein hobby rehne ke liye hi better hoti hain.
And the hardest part? Practising through the phase where it still feels like you're bad at it. That gap between passion and competence is where most people quit and where the real ones stay.

True, talent gives you a start, but it’s practice that helps you grow.With time, effort, and consistency, you slowly improve and get better every day.So we should not stop just because it feels hard we should keep practicing, and will see the change...

The scary part is most people quit right before practice starts feeling like progress. early on it just feels like being bad, repeatedly, with no sign it's working. so they decide they're not meant for it when really they just hadn't stayed long enough to see the gap between where they started and where they actually were now.

When I first tried sound healing, I couldn't hold a singing bowl without my hands shaking. I thought maybe this isn't for me.200+ sessions later, I've helped people cry out stress they'd been carrying for years -- just through vibration and breath.The gap between "I'm bad at this" and "this is my life's work" was about 6 months of embarrassing practice. Raj is spot on.
Most people underestimate how much repetition actually shapes capability over time.
You’re not bad at your passion; you just haven’t practised enough yet. Skill comes from repetition, not talent alone. Give it time, stay consistent, and you’ll surprise yourself.
Raj Shamani Most people quit during the awkward phase where their taste exceeds their current skill The only bridge between the two is practice Keep showing up until the output matches the vision
Most people judge too early. Skill isn’t found, it’s built through repetition long before it feels natural.
What feels like “I’m not good at this” is often just “I haven’t stayed long enough to get good yet.”
People quit too early and call it ‘not my thing.’ Most of the time, it’s just not enough reps yet..
Productivity gurus sell discipline.Biology sells better answers.A 20-minute walk after lunch outperformsany caffeine stack you can build.

Most people treat that 2 pm dip like a motivation problem when it’s really an energy allocation issue.If your day isn’t designed around how your brain actually works, you end up fighting biology instead of using it. Raj Shamani

Swatilekha Das Interesting take, but I'd push back here.Calling it purely an 'energy allocation issue' is just rebranding the same productivity obsession with fancier language.Not everything needs to be optimized. Sometimes the 2 PM slump is your nervous system asking for stillness and not a scheduling problem to engineer away.The moment we start treating our biology like a resource to be 'allocated' efficiently, we've already lost. We're not CPUs. We're not meant to run at peak output with zero waste.The cultures with the longest lifespans don't 'design their days around brain performance.' They eat slowly, rest without guilt, and stop treating rest as something you earn after productivity.Maybe the real hack is... no hack at all.

In today’s episode, we sit down with Ravinder Grover, COO of HarvestPlus Solutions, to talk about nutrition, hidden hunger, and why food today is not as healthy as we think.

We discuss why your roti has lost nutrition, what deficiencies are doing to children, and why so many people are now talking about protein. Ravinder explains hidden hunger, how millions are affected without even realizing it, and the body signals most people ignore.

The conversation also covers how biofortification started, how countries like Kenya tackled iron deficiency, and why India is still lagging behind. He shares why scientists often go unnoticed, the biggest risks ahead, and why one policy decision could change India’s nutrition story.

Hi Raj sir,

I am 16 years old, currently stepping into Class 11 from Pune, Maharashtra.

I come from a family where no one has ever run a business. No one to guide me, no one to ask, no one who understands why a teenage girl is thinking about startups instead of just studying.

But I have an idea. A real one. And I have a fire inside me that I cannot explain.

I have been learning from your content for 1years. You have taught me more about business than anyone in my life ever could. Your videos made me believe that people like me — from small cities, with no connections, no money, no background — can still build something.

I am not asking for investment. I am not asking for hours of your time.

Just 15 minutes. One conversation. A little direction from someone who has walked this path.

I know you receive hundreds of messages. But I also know you started because someone believed in you. I am asking you to be that person for me.

I will not waste your time. I promise.

Thank you for everything you already give for free. It has changed my life.

I’m @finlith , and here’s what I learned from the video:

Aaj ke rice aur wheat me pehle ke comparison me nutrients kaafi kam ho gaye hain.

Soil degradation ek major reason hai (India me ~40% soil zinc-deficient hai)

Seeds ko mainly high yield ke liye banaya ja raha hai, nutrition ignore ho raha hai

Heavy processing bhi food ke nutrients reduce karti hai

Iron deficiency se fatigue aur low focus hota hai

Zinc deficiency se growth aur immunity pe bura effect padta hai

Biofortification ek solution hai — seeds ko naturally zyada nutritious banaya ja raha hai

Sirf protein pe focus karna sahi nahi hai (protein-washing trend misleading hai)

Protein ko body properly use karne ke liye zinc aur iron zaroori hain

Ghar pe nutrition improve karne ke easy ways:
Fermentation se nutrients 2–5x badhte hain
Whole foods use karo, unnecessary peeling avoid karo
Soaking aur sprouting se vitamins unlock hote hain
Mushrooms ko sunlight me rakhne se Vitamin D increase hota hai

Consumer awareness powerful hai — agar log nutrition demand karenge, to companies ko improve karna padega.

transcript: अगर कोई बच्चा नॉर्मल क्रॉप उगा हुआ राइस है, वीट है वह खाता है तो उसके ब्रेन को क्या होता है? सबसे इंपॉर्टेंट चीज आप में जब आयरन नहीं है तो आपको ब्रेन को ब्लड सर्कुलेशन नहीं हो रही है। आपके ऑक्सीजन नहीं जा रही है उसकी। अगर जिंक नहीं है तो आपकी ग्रोथ ही रुक जाती है। एसेंट बच्चे जो पॉसिबल उनकी पोटेंशियल हाइट थी वहां पहुंच ही नहीं पाते हैं। मेंटली भी स्ट्रेंटेड रह जाते हैं। मुझे क्या इंपैक्ट होगा? नाउ दैट आई ग्रोन पर्सन। जैसे आपको आयरन डेफिशिएंसी होती है सबसे पहले आपका फोकस चला जाता है। फिर फटीग आपको दिखेगा जब 50 साल पहले अगर मेरे फादर या मेरे ग्रैंडफादर जब वो रोटी खाते थे उनको 56% जिंक मिलता था उससे। आज आधे से भी कम हो गया। ऐसे क्या हुआ कि जो 50 साल पहले रोटी थी वो न्यूट्रिशियस थी। आज नहीं है। अभी 40% सोइल में इंडिया की जिंक डेफिशिएंसी है। दूसरी क्या है? सोइल में जो लगता है सीड। पुरानी वैरायटीज में बहुत न्यूट्रिशन होता था। अब वाली वैरायटीज में न्यूट्रिशन नहीं है। तीसरी चोरी हो रही है आपकी फैक्ट्री लेवल पे। अब अगर आप देखोगे तो हमारा सब कुछ पैकेट में जा रहा है। तो अल्ट्रा प्रोसेस फ़ूड में क्या होता है कि 30 से 40% न्यूट्रिएंट वहां चले जाते हैं। जो चौथी लास्ट चीज हुई वो हमारी प्लेट में जो सबसे खतरनाक है। पहले अगर आप देखो ना ह्यूमंस 6000 तरह के प्लांट खाते थे। आज अगर देखोगे तो 30 तरह के प्लांट हैं जो मेजर हमारे 70- 80% हमारा खाना है। और अगर इंडिया की बात करो तीन ही ऐसी क्रॉप्स हैं जो हमारी 70% कैलोरीज दे रही हैं। राइस, वीट, मेज़। प्रॉब्लम्स क्या हो रही है सर? अगर आप देखोगे इंडिया में 57% बच्चे हैं जिनको एनीमिया है। 67% वुमेन को एनीमिया है। 35% बच्चे हमारे यहां पे स्टंटेड हैं। ग्रोथ सिर्फ फिजिकल नहीं उनकी मेंटल ग्रोथ भी नहीं हुई है। तो इट्स दिस। अब इसको फिक्स करने के दो तरीके हो सकते हैं। नॉर्मली जब भी हम हेल्थ या फिटनेस की बात करते हैं तो हमारा सारा ध्यान सिर्फ डाइट, कैलोरीज और जिम पर होता है। हमें लगता है कि अगर हमने अपनी प्रोटीन रिक्वायरमेंट्स पूरी कर ली और रोज वर्कआउट कर रहे हैं तो हम परफेक्टली फिट हैं। बट एक बहुत बड़ा गैप है जो हम मिस कर रहे हैं कि कैसे एसेंशियल मिनरल्स हमारे खाने से गायब हो रहे हैं जिसकी वजह से हमारी बॉडी इंटरनली वीक होते जा रही है। हमारे आज के गेस्ट इसी मिसिंग गैप पर काम कर रहे हैं। आई एम टॉकिंग अबाउट रविंद्र ग्रोवर सीओ ऑफ हार्वेस्ट प्लस सॉलशंस। इस कन्वर्सेशन में हम उनसे जानेंगे क्यों आजकल के खाने में जिंक और न्यूट्रिशन कम होता जा रहा है। यह हिडन हंगर क्या है और यह कैसे हमें अंदर से वीक कर रहा है और माइक्रोोनट्रिएंट्स की कमी के अर्ली साइन क्या है जो हम मोस्टली इग्नोर कर देते हैं। टू नो मोर अबाउट हार्वेस्ट प्लस सॉलशंस एंड द न्यूट्री पाठशाला इनिशिएटिव चेक द लिंक इन द डिस्क्रिप्शन। सो इन सिंपल वर्ड्स टेल मी मेन काम क्या है? सबसे पहले तो इंडिया में अभी क्या चल रहा है? हम लोगों 330 मिलियन टन्स हम फूड प्रोड्यूस कर रहे हैं आज की डेट में और फूड टेस्टी भी है। हमने काफी अच्छी प्रोसेसिंग कर ली। उसको चमका भी लिया। सुंदर भी दिखता है आजकल फूड। 10 मिनट में आ भी जाता है। हम तो मोटा-मोटी फूड सिक्योर तो हम हैं। लेकिन एक जो सबसे बड़ी प्लेट प्रॉब्लम चल रही है वो है डे लाइट रॉबरी न्यूट्रिशन हस्ट। हमारी प्लेट से जब हम जुम्बा कर रहे थे, जिम जा रहे थे, प्रोटीन शेक पी रहे थे, कोई पीछे से आया, हमारी प्लेट से न्यूट्रिशन ही चोरी करके ले गया। तो वो एक सबसे बड़ी प्रॉब्लम है। हम और वो हम ट्राई कर रहे हैं सॉल्व करने का। मैं जैसे एग्जांपल देता हूं आपको। 50 साल पहले अगर मेरे फादर या मेरे ग्रैंडफादर जब वो रोटी खाते थे उनको 56% जिंक मिलता था उससे आज कितना मिलता होगा आप गेस करो सेम रोटी 10% 20% 25% हां तो हो गया आधा आधे से भी कम हो गया सेम स्टोरी है राइस की उसमें जिंक आयरन की सेम सिचुएशन है 30% तक वो कम हो गया मतलब अगर मेरे को सेम न्यूट्रिशन लेना है आज तो मेरे को डबल रोटी खाना पड़ेगा। फिर हम बोलते हैं कार्ब्स आर इवल। तो कार्ब्स तो इवल हो ही गए क्योंकि कैलोरीज ही बचा है सारा कुछ। उसमें से हमने न्यूट्रिशन तो उड़ा दिया। तो ये जो रॉबरी चल रही है और मैंने तो किसी को हेल्थ का ओटीपी दिया भी नहीं था। कोई फिर भी ले गया चुरा के मेरी न्यूट्रिशन। तो ये जो रॉबरी चल रही है और इसके पीछे जो चोर हैं उनको पकड़ने की कहानी है जो हम कर रहे हैं। बताओ क्या हुआ? ऐसे क्या हुआ कि जो 50 साल पहले रोटी थी वो न्यूट्रिशियस थी। आज नहीं है। चार मेन कारण है। चार चार जगह पे चोरी हो रही है। शुरू से शुरू करते हैं। हम खाना बनता है। सोइल से शुरू होती है हमारी जर्नी। सोइल में हम सीड डालते

Hi Raj sir,

I am 16 years old, currently stepping into Class 11 from Pune, Maharashtra.

I come from a family where no one has ever run a business. No one to guide me, no one to ask, no one who understands why a teenage girl is thinking about startups instead of just studying.

But I have an idea. A real one. And I have a fire inside me that I cannot explain.

I have been learning from your content for 1years. You have taught me more about business than anyone in my life ever could. Your videos made me believe that people like me — from small cities, with no connections, no money, no background — can still build something.

I am not asking for investment. I am not asking for hours of your time.

Just 15 minutes. One conversation. A little direction from someone who has walked this path.

I know you receive hundreds of messages. But I also know you started because someone believed in you. I am asking you to be that person for me.

I will not waste your time. I promise.
commentsTranslated.5:
I'm @finlith , and here's what I learned from the video:

Today's rice and wheat have significantly fewer nutrients compared to before.

Soil degradation is a major reason (in India, ~40% of soil is zinc-deficient)

Seeds are mainly being made for high yield, nutrition is being ignored

Heavy processing also reduces the nutrients in food

Iron deficiency causes fatigue and low focus

Zinc deficiency has a bad effect on growth and immunity

Biofortification is a solution — seeds are being naturally made more nutritious

Focusing only on protein isn't right (the protein-washing trend is misleading)

For the body to properly use protein, zinc and iron are essential

Easy ways to improve nutrition at home:
Fermentation increases nutrients 2–5x
Use whole foods, avoid unnecessary peeling
Soaking and sprouting unlock vitamins
Keeping mushrooms in sunlight increases Vitamin D

Consumer awareness pow

Hi Raj sir,

I am 16 years old, currently stepping into Class 11 from Pune, Maharashtra.

I come from a family where no one has ever run a business. No one to guide me, no one to ask, no one who understands why a teenage girl is thinking about startups instead of just studying.

But I have an idea. A real one. And I have a fire inside me that I cannot explain.

I have been learning from your content for 1 years. You have taught me more about business than anyone in my life ever could. Your videos made me believe that people like me — from small cities, with no connections, no money, no background — can still build something.

I am not asking for investment. I am not asking for hours of your time.

Just 15 minutes. One conversation. A little direction from someone who has walked this path.

I know you receive hundreds of messages. But I also know you started because someone believed in you. I am asking you to be that person for me.

I will not waste your time. I promise.

hook_text_english: Relying on mood to decide actions gives you less control over your life.
full_text_english: The more you rely on your mood to decide your actions, the less control you have over your life.Most people live their entire lives based on how they feel when they wake up. Like if the mood is good, they will work out, they will show up, they will do the hard thing. And if the mood is off, they'll push everything to tomorrow. The problem with this whole thing is that feelings are unreliable. Like they change based on what you ate, how you slept, one conversation that went wrong, a message you read at the wrong time. You don't know if your mood will be good the next day as well. And you're letting that decide whether you show up for yourself or not on any given day. What you need to do is stop asking "do I feel like doing this" and just start doing it because it was on the list. Like it sounds almost stupidly simple, but that is really the difference between you and someone who is exceptionally good at what they do. Over time, the gap becomes so wide that it is absurd. Your mood is going to lie to you almost every morning. It is going to tell you that today is not the day, that you are tired, that you will start fresh next week. And if you keep listening to it, you will have lost your years to days where nothing was actually wrong, you just did not feel like it. hashtag#rajshamani hashtag#figuringout
body_summary_english: Explains how mood-dependent behavior undermines life control since feelings are unreliable and fluctuate based on sleep, diet, conversations, and random messages.
comments: i’ve noticed the days i don’t “feel like it” but still show up are the ones i’m most proud of later. this is a good reminder that consistency isn’t emotional, it’s intentional.
Sakshi Pandey 👍

Discipline isn’t about ignoring mood—it’s about not negotiating with it every morning.People who move ahead don’t feel better, they just decide less.Consistency comes from removing the daily debate, not winning it. Raj Shamani
Swatilekha Das "Stop asking 'do I feel like it?' and just do it." That's the whole game right there.

This also explains why people feel inconsistent even when they’re capable. They’re not lacking ability Raj, they’re just letting a fluctuating internal state decide a fixed external goal. And those two will never align consistently.
Exactly Mohsin Nathani, it doesn’t feel like much in the moment, but it adds up over years.

This hits hard because most people don’t realize how much inconsistency compounds over time.One thing I’ve noticed is that the highest performers don’t rely on discipline alone they build systems that make showing up automatic, regardless of mood. When the process is predefined, the decision is already made.Curious how others approach this do you rely more on willpower, or have you built systems that remove the need for it?

Discipline matters, but ignoring mood completely isn’t realistic either. The real edge comes from building systems where action happens regardless of how you feel, while still managing energy so you don’t burn out.Consistency is not about fighting yourself every day, it’s about reducing the need to.

Discipline over mood is the real game changer. Feelings fluctuate, but consistent action builds momentum and results. The people who move ahead aren’t the most motivated—they’re the most reliable with their habits. Show up anyway, especially on the days you don’t feel like it. That’s where real growth begins.

Mood is a terrible manager.It negotiates. Discipline executes.Most people are waiting to feel ready.The ones who win just follow the plan — especially when they don’t feel like it.Consistency isn’t emotional. It’s scheduled.
discipline removes the need to negotiate with your mood.Feelings fluctuate, but systems keep you consistent.Long-term success comes from showing up regardless 💯

Discipline really is about showing up despite how you feel, not because of it. I’ve noticed that the biggest progress comes from the average days the ones where motivation is low but you still stick to the plan. That’s where consistency is built. Mood can guide you, but it shouldn’t be the decision-maker. Systems > feelings, every time.
Treat your mood like a chatty intern.Listen briefly. Approve zero.Show up anyway
Pawel M. Pawlak, PhD Outsourcing decisions to moods is like hiring weather as a CFO.
The absurd gap you mention isn't just about output. It's about compound trust in your own word. That self-trust is the real asset.
Discipline is just pre-deciding your actions before emotions get a vote. The people who scale aren’t more motivated, they’re just less negotiable with themselves.

transcript: Focus is becoming the rarest skill on the planet. You have the talent, you have the intelligence, you have the best tools, but none of it matters if you can't stay focused on one task. Because the person who's slightly less talented than you, but can sit with one problem for hours straight is going to beat you every single time. Your success isn't about what you know. It's about how long you can hold your attention to one useful thing. And you know why most people fail at that? Because real focus is boring. It's repetitive. It's relentless. And if you can't control your attention, you can't win. Stop looking for a shortcut. Start controlling your focus.

hook_text_english: It's weird how you stop feeling things the way you used to.
full_text_english: it’s weird how you stop feeling things the way you used to.things you once wanted so badly, worked so hard forbut when you get them, it just feels normal.no big high.no crazy excitement.just okay and, your brain’s already asking what’s next?and for a second you think something’s wrong. that you aren’t appreciating things enough. but maybe nothing is wrong.maybe you just outgrew the idea that achievements are supposed to make you feel complete.maybe it was always about the grind.the late nights.the growth.the version of you that was being built.and so now you don’t chase the moment anymore.you just learned to fall in love with becoming the person who is capable of achieving those things...Styled by @reannmoradian Team @sakshichitalia
body_summary_english: The post explores how achievements lose their emotional impact over time, suggesting that fulfillment comes from growth and the grind rather than the destination itself.

caption: it’s weird how you stop feeling things the way you used to.things you once wanted so badly, worked so hard forbut when you get them, it just feels normal.no big high.no crazy excitement.just okay and, your brain’s already asking what’s next?and for a second you think something’s wrong. that you aren’t appreciating things enough. but maybe nothing is wrong.maybe you just outgrew the idea that achievements are supposed to make you feel complete.maybe it was always about the grind.the late nights.the growth.the version of you that was being built.and so now you don’t chase the moment anymore.you just learned to fall in love with becoming the person who is capable of achieving those things...Styled by @reannmoradian Team @sakshichitalia

hook_text_english: 95,000 Indians died from falling in 2004, number growing
full_text_english: In 2004, 95,000 Indians died from falling. That number has only grown since.In our recent episode I talked to Prashant Desai, a health educator who studied at Stanford School of Medicine, about why falling is one of the biggest killers of Indians after 65.He explained that what determines whether you survive a fall is your muscle.Muscle is what catches you. Muscle is what wraps around your bones. Muscle is the only thing standing between you and a fracture.And the biggest misconception is that you don’t start to lose muscle in your old age.You start losing it at the age of 25, 1% every year. So by the time you fall at an elderly age, the damage is already years old.The issue behind all of this is that, on average, Indians eat less than half the protein their body needs.And when the body does not get enough protein from food, it takes it from your own muscles.Full episode out now, watch it here: https://lnkd.in/d27J2uifThis episode is delivered by Porter.hashtag#rajshamani hashtag#figuringout
body_summary_english: Episode reveals that muscle loss starting at age 25 is critical to fall survival, making it a major health concern for elderly Indians.
comments: This highlights something most people ignore — health isn’t lost suddenly, it declines silently over years. By the time the problem shows up, the root cause has already been building for decades.Muscle isn’t just about fitness or aesthetics, it’s a long-term investment in resilience, mobility, and independence. The real shift is treating it as essential, not optional.
Neeru Yadav Hockey Wali Sarpanch "Muscle catches you." That visual will stick with me. Important episode.

Most people treat muscle as aesthetics.It’s actually insurance.Strength isn’t for the gym—it’s for the fall you didn’t plan for.Neglect compounds quietly.So does discipline.Train now, or pay later—with interest.
his hit hard. We talk so much about heart disease and diabetes but almost nobody talks about muscle loss as a silent killer. The fact that it starts at 25 and we don't even notice is genuinely scary.

Most people don’t consciously choose validation over goals, they drift into it. The moment feedback becomes instant and visible, it slowly replaces progress as the scoreboard. By the time you notice, you’re optimizing for applause, not outcomes.

This really puts things into perspective.We often associate strength with aesthetics, but it’s actually long-term insurance for mobility and survival. The fact that muscle loss starts so early makes it even more important to act before it feels urgent.
Raj Shamani Clarity rarely shows up before action, it’s a byproduct of it. Most people wait to “figure it out,” but the ones who move first are the ones who actually do.
Most people wait to have life figured out before moving…but “figuring it out” is what movement was always for.Clarity rarely comes first.Courage usually does.
This is such an important reminder—health isn’t built in old age, it’s built daily over decades.Strength, nutrition, and small consistent habits today are what protect us in the long run.

This post highlights a critical failure in how we manage our physical infrastructure. We often view health passively, but survival into later years requires a strictly logic-driven, structural approach. Muscle is not an aesthetic feature; it is the load-bearing foundation that absorbs impact and prevents catastrophic structural failure in the body.However, looking at physiological facts, protein deficiency is only half the equation. Even with optimal protein intake, muscles will still atrophy without mechanical tension. The body actively discards tissue it is not forced to use. Sarcopenia (age-related muscle loss) is driven equally by a profound lack of load-bearing resistance training.Regarding the Indian diet, the solution is not to abandon our domestic culinary habits. The innovation lies in structurally optimising them. We can logically re-engineer traditional Indian, predominantly vegetarian, home cooking to meet functional protein requirements without losing its cultural essence.Expand this narrative to include the absolute necessity of resistance training. Protein provides the raw materials, but physical resistance provides the actual blueprint for the body to build the wall.

Reading this honestly hits home. We rarely think about muscle, protein, or strength in our 20s and 30s — until much later when the damage quietly shows up. A simple reminder that taking care of our body today is actually a gift to our future self.

Watched the whole podcast, really liked how simply it was explained.Didn’t realise how most of us are actually eating way less protein than we need. That part about 0.8 g/kg just being the minimum and not really enough for muscle and overall health was eye-opening. Also made sense why people feel low energy or struggle with weight even after eating full meals.The breakdown on things like leucine, muscle loss with age, and why protein matters so much for strength and metabolism was honestly something I hadn’t thought about before. And yeah, good that it cleared the usual myths too… protein isn’t the problem, we’re just not getting enough of it in the first place.

Yeh point kaafi log ignore kar dete hain ki muscle loss itna early start ho jata hai. Lekin sirf protein ko blame karna thoda oversimplified lagta hai - activity level, strength training aur lifestyle bhi utna hi important role play karte hain.Real issue shayad yeh hai ki hum preventive health ko seriously hi nahi lete jab tak problem visible na ho.

hook_text_english: Soft skills will make you more money than most hard skills in the next decade.
full_text_english: Soft skills will make you more money than most hard skills in the next decade. The person in the room with the better resume will almost always lose to the person who can make you trust them in ten minutes. And this makes people who have spent years perfecting hard skills furious because it feels unfair. It is. That is the point.Competence gets you into the room. The ability to make people feel something is what closes it. Reading people, adjusting your energy, knowing when to speak and when to shut up, making the other person feel like the most important one in the conversation, there is no certification for any of this. We have built entire education systems around hard skills. Degrees, courses, bootcamps, certifications, all designed to make you more technically qualified. And the person who masters communication and learns how to make a room trust them will outearn most of them over a lifetime. Money follows trust before it follows talent. It always has.The next decade is going to make this gap wider. AI is closing the hard skills distance faster than anyone expected. The things people spent years learning are becoming commodities in months. But the ability to sit across from someone, hold a conversation that matters, and walk away with their belief, that is going to be the last skill left that cannot be automated.hashtag#rajshamani hashtag#figuringout
body_summary_english: The post argues that interpersonal and emotional intelligence matter more than technical expertise, and that the ability to build trust and read people is what converts opportunities.

Watched the whole podcast, really liked how simply it was explained.Didn’t realise how most of us are actually eating way less protein than we need. That part about 0.8 g/kg just being the minimum and not really enough for muscle and overall health was eye-opening. Also made sense why people feel low energy or struggle with weight even after eating full meals.The breakdown on things like leucine, muscle loss with age, and why protein matters so much for strength and metabolism was honestly something I hadn’t thought about before. And yeah, good that it cleared the usual myths too… protein isn’t the problem, we’re just not getting enough of it in the first place.

Yeh point kaafi log ignore kar dete hain ki muscle loss itna early start ho jata hai. Lekin sirf protein ko blame karna thoda oversimplified lagta hai - activity level, strength training aur lifestyle bhi utna hi important role play karte hain.Real issue shayad yeh hai ki hum preventive health ko seriously hi nahi lete jab tak problem visible na ho.
Never thought muscle = life insurance 😳We focus so much on looks, but strength is actually survival.This really changed my perspective.
Raj ShamaniImportant reminder. Strength isn’t just about fitness, it’s about long-term resilience and independence. What you build early quietly compounds over decades.

Important message. Healthy aging doesn’t begin at 65 it begins decades earlier. Strength, movement, and proper nutrition are long-term investments that protect independence and quality of life later on.
dad keeps saying “eat properly” like a broken record never connected it to this… thought gym is enough most days protein just gets ignored without noticing
Most people wait too long. Muscle is long-term insurance—you can’t rush it at 65.

Honestly, we tracked a B2B client's productivity dip last quarter. Low protein intake was the hidden variable.

Raj Shamani this is such an underrated insight—people think health issues start later, but the reality is they compound silently for decades 🧠 Muscle isn’t just about fitness, it’s long-term protection. What you do in your 20s and 30s directly decides how strong you’ll be in your 60s 💯

Critical awareness that needs wider attention. Prevention through education and better safety infrastructure can save thousands of lives every year.

This is genuinely life-saving info. Half the protein needed is wild. Sharing this. Raj Shamani

Consistency matters, but calling it “laziness” oversimplifies the reality. Skills don’t disappear — they just go dormant without use. The real difference lies in how quickly you choose to get back and rebuild them.
Neeru Yadav Hockey Wali Sarpanch "Didn't lose talent, just got lazy." Harsh but true. Needed this slap.

Skill loss mindset:* “I lost my ability”* “I’m not as good anymore”* Avoid restartingBetter mindset:* “I’m just out of reps”* “It’ll come back with practice”* Restart quicklySkills don’t disappear.They go dormant.
Zaki Akhtar That “out of reps” framing is spot on.Most of the time it’s not that the skill is gone, it’s just unused for a while. Starting again feels hard, but it comes back quicker than you expect.
caption: Soft skills will make you more money than most hard skills in the next decade. The person in the room with the better resume will almost always lose to the person who can make you trust them in ten minutes. And this makes people who have spent years perfecting hard skills furious because it feels unfair. It is. That is the point.Competence gets you into the room. The ability to make people feel something is what closes it. Reading people, adjusting your energy, knowing when to speak and when to shut up, making the other person feel like the most important one in the conversation, there is no certification for any of this. We have built entire education systems around hard skills. Degrees, courses, bootcamps, certifications, all designed to make you more technically qualified. And the person who masters communication and learns how to make a room trust them will outearn most of them over a lifetime. Money follows trust before it follows talent. It always has.The next decade is going to make this gap wider. AI is closing the hard skills distance faster than anyone expected. The things people spent years learning are becoming commodities in months. But the ability to sit across from someone, hold a conversation that matters, and walk away with their belief, that is going to be the last skill left that cannot be automated.hashtag#rajshamani hashtag#figuringout

transcript: Whoever controls your money controls your life. If you depend on your boss to survive, you stay silent when they disrespect you. If you depend on your parents, you agree just to keep peace. If you depend on your partner, you tolerate what you shouldn't. And this isn't about love or loyalty. This is fear. Fear of where you will go, what you will do, how will you survive if you don't obey. Money lets you move on. Money lets you leave the job, leave the person, leave the place. You don't need money just for a luxury life. You need it so you can live life on your own terms. The real power of money is not having to ask anyone for permission. It's being able to say no and walk away without thinking twice. Money doesn't buy you happiness, but money helps you get freedom, gets you options, gets you control, and that gives you happiness.

hook_text_english: Do you know how much protein your body actually needs daily?
full_text_english: Comment ‘Protein’ and I’ll personally DM you the full conversation.Do you know how much protein your body actually needs daily?In today’s episode, our guest is @itsprashantdesai, Health Educator Stanford School of Medicine to talk about the real role of protein in the Indian diet, why protein deficiency is more common than we think, and how it impacts hunger, cravings, and overall health.Here’s what we discussed:-Why most Indians are unknowingly under-eating protein even in “full” meals-The hidden link between low protein intake and constant snacking/cravings-How protein influences metabolism, fat loss, and muscle preservation-The truth about “too much protein” and kidney health (myth vs science)-Simple, realistic ways to increase protein in a typical Indian thaliCatch the full episode here:YouTube Channel: Raj ShamaniSpotify Channel: Raj Shamani Figuring OutUntil then, keep figuring out! 🚀#rajshamani #figuringout

comments: This highlights something most people ignore — health isn’t lost suddenly, it declines silently over years. By the time the problem shows up, the root cause has already been building for decades.Muscle isn’t just about fitness or aesthetics, it’s a long-term investment in resilience, mobility, and independence. The real shift is treating it as essential, not optional.
Neeru Yadav Hockey Wali Sarpanch "Muscle catches you." That visual will stick with me. Important episode.

Most people treat muscle as aesthetics.It’s actually insurance.Strength isn’t for the gym—it’s for the fall you didn’t plan for.Neglect compounds quietly.So does discipline.Train now, or pay later—with interest.
his hit hard. We talk so much about heart disease and diabetes but almost nobody talks about muscle loss as a silent killer. The fact that it starts at 25 and we don't even notice is genuinely scary.

Most people don’t consciously choose validation over goals, they drift into it. The moment feedback becomes instant and visible, it slowly replaces progress as the scoreboard. By the time you notice, you’re optimizing for applause, not outcomes.

This really puts things into perspective.We often associate strength with aesthetics, but it’s actually long-term insurance for mobility and survival. The fact that muscle loss starts so early makes it even more important to act before it feels urgent.
Raj Shamani Clarity rarely shows up before action, it’s a byproduct of it. Most people wait to “figure it out,” but the ones who move first are the ones who actually do.
Most people wait to have life figured out before moving…but “figuring it out” is what movement was always for.Clarity rarely comes first.Courage usually does.
This is such an important reminder—health isn’t built in old age, it’s built daily over decades.Strength, nutrition, and small consistent habits today are what protect us in the long run.

This post highlights a critical failure in how we manage our physical infrastructure. We often view health passively, but survival into later years requires a strictly logic-driven, structural approach. Muscle is not an aesthetic feature; it is the load-bearing foundation that absorbs impact and prevents catastrophic structural failure in the body.However, looking at physiological facts, protein deficiency is only half the equation. Even with optimal protein intake, muscles will still atrophy without mechanical tension. The body actively discards tissue it is not forced to use. Sarcopenia (age-related muscle loss) is driven equally by a profound lack of load-bearing resistance training.Regarding the Indian diet, the solution is not to abandon our domestic culinary habits. The innovation lies in structurally optimising them. We can logically re-engineer traditional Indian, predominantly vegetarian, home cooking to meet functional protein requirements without losing its cultural essence.Expand this narrative to include the absolute necessity of resistance training. Protein provides the raw materials, but physical resistance provides the actual blueprint for the body to build the wall.

Reading this honestly hits home. We rarely think about muscle, protein, or strength in our 20s and 30s — until much later when the damage quietly shows up. A simple reminder that taking care of our body today is actually a gift to our future self.

Watched the whole podcast, really liked how simply it was explained.Didn’t realise how most of us are actually eating way less protein than we need. That part about 0.8 g/kg just being the minimum and not really enough for muscle and overall health was eye-opening. Also made sense why people feel low energy or struggle with weight even after eating full meals.The breakdown on things like leucine, muscle loss with age, and why protein matters so much for strength and metabolism was honestly something I hadn’t thought about before. And yeah, good that it cleared the usual myths too… protein isn’t the problem, we’re just not getting enough of it in the first place.

Yeh point kaafi log ignore kar dete hain ki muscle loss itna early start ho jata hai. Lekin sirf protein ko blame karna thoda oversimplified lagta hai - activity level, strength training aur lifestyle bhi utna hi important role play karte hain.Real issue shayad yeh hai ki hum preventive health ko seriously hi nahi lete jab tak problem visible na ho.
Never thought muscle = life insurance 😳We focus so much on looks, but strength is actually survival.This really changed my perspective.
Raj ShamaniImportant reminder. Strength isn’t just about fitness, it’s about long-term resilience and independence. What you build early quietly compounds over decades.

Important message. Healthy aging doesn’t begin at 65 it begins decades earlier. Strength, movement, and proper nutrition are long-term investments that protect independence and quality of life later on.
dad keeps saying “eat properly” like a broken record never connected it to this… thought gym is enough most days protein just gets ignored without noticing
Most people wait too long. Muscle is long-term insurance—you can’t rush it at 65.

hook_text_english: Paul brought a 15-foot Burmese python into the studio, and I was scared to death.
full_text_english: Paul brought a 15-foot Burmese python into the studio, and I was scared to death.It wrapped around my shoulder, and as much as I was dying on the inside, the snake looked mesmerising. It was half on Paul's shoulders and half on mine, yet the weight was still too heavy. Paul looked at me and said, "She's not trying to hurt you. She's just trying to find somewhere safe."This is our 500th episode of Figuring Out. And it's the first time we've had a live animal on set.Paul Rosolie is a conservationist and author who has spent 20 years protecting 136,000 acres of the Amazon rainforest. He's lived inside the Amazon for years, building Junglekeepers, protecting the rainforest, tracking animals. He's also spent time in various jungles of the world, including India's Western Ghats. He's also the person whom the narcos in the Amazon want dead. They're burning down forests to grow narcotic dr*gs, and Paul's team, the Junglekeepers, are the ones flying drones, tracking their movements, working with rangers to stop them. So they sent out a message to their network: if you see Paul or JJ, shoot them. A cop who helped them escape one of these chases was murdered later the same day.He told me he spent a year and a half in sheer terror after this became real. Now he travels with a security detail. And yet, this is someone who's devoted his life to protecting butterflies, birds, snakes. Helpless species being wiped off the planet. He said, "It's kind of funny that you could devote yourself to something so pure, and then there's humans that want to k*ll you for doing that."We talked about being charged by wild elephants in India, why snakes avoid humans, and most bites happen because people panic, and why wildlife trafficking is the 4th largest black market in the world, $23 billion a year. 500 episodes in, but it's still day 1. Bigger, bolder, and better stuff is coming your way 🫡 Full episode out now, watch it here: https://lnkd.in/dKmGf-9Ghashtag#rajshamani hashtag#figuringout
body_summary_english: A milestone episode (500) of Figuring Out podcast features conservationist Paul Rosolie and a live Burmese python, creating an intimate moment about trust and nature. The episode highlights Rosolie's 20-year conservation work protecting 136,000 acres of Amazon rainforest.

This is genuinely life-saving info. Half the protein needed is wild. Sharing this. Raj Shamani
Therefore consuming milk and nutritious foods helps keep our body and muscles strong💪🏻

Consistency matters, but calling it “laziness” oversimplifies the reality. Skills don’t disappear — they just go dormant without use. The real difference lies in how quickly you choose to get back and rebuild them.
Neeru Yadav Hockey Wali Sarpanch "Didn't lose talent, just got lazy." Harsh but true. Needed this slap.

Skill loss mindset:* “I lost my ability”* “I’m not as good anymore”* Avoid restartingBetter mindset:* “I’m just out of reps”* “It’ll come back with practice”* Restart quicklySkills don’t disappear.They go dormant.
caption: Paul brought a 15-foot Burmese python into the studio, and I was scared to death.It wrapped around my shoulder, and as much as I was dying on the inside, the snake looked mesmerising. It was half on Paul's shoulders and half on mine, yet the weight was still too heavy. Paul looked at me and said, "She's not trying to hurt you. She's just trying to find somewhere safe."This is our 500th episode of Figuring Out. And it's the first time we've had a live animal on set.Paul Rosolie is a conservationist and author who has spent 20 years protecting 136,000 acres of the Amazon rainforest. He's lived inside the Amazon for years, building Junglekeepers, protecting the rainforest, tracking animals. He's also spent time in various jungles of the world, including India's Western Ghats. He's also the person whom the narcos in the Amazon want dead. They're burning down forests to grow narcotic dr*gs, and Paul's team, the Junglekeepers, are the ones flying drones, tracking their movements, working with rangers to stop them. So they sent out a message to their network: if you see Paul or JJ, shoot them. A cop who helped them escape one of these chases was murdered later the same day.He told me he spent a year and a half in sheer terror after this became real. Now he travels with a security detail. And yet, this is someone who's devoted his life to protecting butterflies, birds, snakes. Helpless species being wiped off the planet. He said, "It's kind of funny that you could devote yourself to something so pure, and then there's humans that want to k*ll you for doing that."We talked about being charged by wild elephants in India, why snakes avoid humans, and most bites happen because people panic, and why wildlife trafficking is the 4th largest black market in the world, $23 billion a year. 500 episodes in, but it's still day 1. Bigger, bolder, and better stuff is coming your way 🫡 Full episode out now, watch it here: https://lnkd.in/dKmGf-9Ghashtag#rajshamani hashtag#figuringout

In this episode, we sit down with Prashant Desai, Health Educator Stanford School of Medicine.

We discuss the real role of protein in the Indian diet, why protein deficiency is more common than we think, and how it impacts hunger, cravings, and overall health.

The conversation also explores common myths around high-protein diets, whether excess protein affects kidney function in healthy individuals, and how traditional Indian diets often fall short in protein adequacy.

He also breaks down research-backed insights on overeating, appetite regulation, and how the body responds when protein intake is low, along with practical ways to improve daily nutrition without extreme diets or supplements. This episode offers a clear, science-based understanding of protein, diet behaviour, and long-term health.

Subscribe for more such conversations.

transcript: इंडियन खाने का एक प्रॉब्लम है। सो जाओ इंडियन की थाली देखोगे 10 ग्राम प्रोटीन के लिए आपको इतनी रोटी खानी पड़ेगी। नौ रोटियां 10 ग्राम प्रोटीन ग्राम के लिए। ये 100 ग्राम दाल के अंदर में कितना प्रोटीन होगा सोचिए? 24 ग्राम। लेकिन ये 24 ग्राम के लिए मुझे ये पूरी दाल खानी पड़ेगी। स्ट्रक्चरली सी योर कैलोरीज फ्रॉम प्रोटीन इज लेस देन 10%। आज हमारे साथ है प्रशांत देसाई जो एक हेल्थ एजुकेटर है। प्रोटीन मैं मेरी बॉडी में बढ़ा देता हूं। मेरा वेट लॉस हो जाएगा। रॉबिन हाइमरी 360 कॉकरोचेस लिए और यह सारे कॉकरोचेस को बेसिकली दो बकेट्स में डिफरेंट डाइट थे। एक सेट ऑफ कॉकरोचेस को इन्होंने हाई प्रोटीन लो कार्ब फूड दिया और एक सेट ऑफ कॉकरोचेस को हाई कार्ब लो प्रोटीन दिया। जिस कॉकरोचेस को इन्होंने हाई कार्ब लो प्रोटीन दी उन्होंने ज्यादा खाया। ये कॉकरोच मोटे हो गए। दीज़ टेस्ट्स वर आल्सो डन इन यूएनआई। दे आल्सो सॉ ह्यूमंस ओवर ईट टिल देयर मिनिमम प्रोटीन रिक्वायरमेंट्स आर मेड। इंटरेस्टिंग। इस एपिसोड में हम बात करेंगे प्रोटीन की रियलेंस। इंडियन डाइट की रियलिटी और कैसे राइट प्रोटीन आपकी हेल्थ और वेट दोनों को ट्रांसफॉर्म कर सकता है। सो अगर आप वो लोग हो जिसको प्रोटीन बढ़ाना है और कैलोरीज को कम करना है तो आप सोयाबीन को अगर आप फ्रेंड बनाओगे 150 से 200 कैलोरी के बीच में आपको 15 से 16 ग्राम प्रोटीन मिल जाता है। लो फैट पनीर मिलता है। उसमें आपको 23 ग्राम प्रोटीन मिल जाएगा। सिर्फ 150 कैलोरीज में। अगर आप अपनी डाइट, फिटनेस और लॉन्ग टर्म हेल्थ इंप्रूव करना चाहते हो तो यह एपिसोड एंड तक जरूर देखना। बट हाउ मच प्रोटीन इजेंट? WHO रेकमेंड करते हैं 0.8 ग्राम्स पर केजी ऑफ़ बॉडी वेट। तो अगर राज आपका वेट से 70 kg है तो आपको 84 85 ग्राम्स दिन का प्रोटीन खाना चाहिए। प्रोटीन शेक्स? टेल मी इज इट हेल्दी? सबको लगता है किडनी में स्टोन हो जाता है। टू थिंग्स आई वांट टू से। डस ईटिंग एक्सेस प्रोटीन रिजल्ट इन डैमेज टू द किडनी? देयर इज नो एविडेंस व्हाट सो एवर। डू पीपल हूस किडनी इज ऑलरेडी डैमेज? शुड दे हैव एक्सेस प्रोटीन? आंसर टु दैट इज अब्सोलुटली। अगर आपको कोई सामान भेजना हो या कहीं से मंगवाना हो बहुत बार ऐसा होता है कि सही ऑप्शन मिलना मुश्किल हो जाता है। एंड फॉर स्मॉलर आइटम्स इट्स ऑलवेज अ स्ट्रगल। इसीलिए आई सजेस्ट पोर्टर। इनके टू व्हीलर्स कैन इजीली हैंडल अप टू 20 kg एंड द बेस्ट पार्ट इज कि ऐप पर जो प्राइस दिखता है वही फाइनल प्राइस होता है। आपको रेट्स के लिए नेगोशिएट नहीं करना पड़ता और ना ही इनके पार्टनर्स आपसे कभी एक्स्ट्रा कैश मांगेंगे। जब एक रिलायबल एक्सपर्ट आपका सामान ले जा रहा हो तो आप टेंशन फ्री होकर अपने काम पर फोकस कर सकते हैं। टू नो मोर अबाउट पोर्टर, क्लिक द लिंक इन द डिस्क्रिप्शन। वेलकम बैक। अरे थैंक यू आई सी यू मोर इन देन मी मोरेंटली यार आई आई आई ऑलवेज आई वांटेड टू थैंक यू पर्सनली यार कि जब हम प्रोटीन की बात कर रहे थे आपने सोचा कि मेरे साथ प्रोटीन डिस्कस करेंगे सो आपकी और आपकी टीम का बिग थैंक यू डीपली ऑनर्ड कि ये सब्जेक्ट इज वेरी क्लोज टू माय हार्ट इसलिए आई एम आई एम आई एम थैंकिंग यू तो मैं हमेशा बोलता हूं मैं घर में भी बात करता हूं अमिताभ बच्चन शुरू शुरू करता है ना चलिए खेलते हैं कौन बनेगा करोड़पति तो मैं कोई भी न्यूट्रिशनल कॉन्फ्रेंस में जाता हूं मैं बोलता हूं चलिए खेलते हैं कौन बनेगा प्रोटीनपति सो इतना इंपॉर्टेंट है मेरे लिए प्रोटीन राज क्यों इंपॉर्टेंट है प्रोटीन आपके लिए सो स्ट्रक्चरली बोलूं मेरे लिए भी फ्रॉम तीन थ्री रीज़ंस है एक पेट्रियोटिक है एक पर्सपेक्टिव रीजन है और एक पर्सनल रीजन है। पेट्रियोटिक जितना मैंने प्रोटीन के बारे में पढ़ा है लास्ट 101 सालों में मेरा यह बहुत बड़ी मेरी बिलीफ है कि जिस दिन इंडिया प्रोटीन के एडिक्वसी और ऑप्टिमम लेवल में पहुंच जाएगी ना हमारे मेजर हेल्थ प्रॉब्लम्स अपने आप सॉल्व हो जाएंगे। चाहे आप ओबेसिटी की बात कीजिए, चाहे आप डायबिटीज की बात कीजिए। एंड बोथ ओबेसिटी एंड डायबिटीज आर एक्सलरेटर्स फॉर योर टॉप फाइव किलर्स इन इंडिया। हार्ट अटैक एंड स्ट्रोक, कैंसर, अल्जाइमर, ऑर्गन फेलियर ये ओबेसिटी डायबिटी का जो मिलन है यह सारी चीज को एक्सलरेट कर देता है। राइट? तो अगर मैंने ओबेसिटी और डायबिटीज सॉल्व कर ली, मेरा ये पर्सनल मानना है कि आज हम इंडिया में ऑन एवरेज 7072 इयर्स जी रहे हैं। ओके? इफ यू लुक एट सिंगापुर, हांगकांग, द टॉप लिविंग कंट्रीज दे आर एट 85 इयर्स। तो बिकॉज़ आप इंडिया में पैदा हुए आप 12-15 साल का तो टैक्स ऐसे ही पे कर रहे हो कि आप कम जीने वाले हो। एंड इसके पीछे जब आप डबल क्लिक करोगे इज बिकॉज़ हमारी जो लाइफस्टाइल हैबिट्स बन गई है ऐसी कि आप इंडिया में हो तो देय

In this episode, we sit down with Prashant Desai, Health Educator Stanford School of Medicine.

We discuss the real role of protein in the Indian diet, why protein deficiency is more common than we think, and how it impacts hunger, cravings, and overall health.

The conversation also explores common myths around high-protein diets, whether excess protein affects kidney function in healthy individuals, and how traditional Indian diets often fall short in protein adequacy.

He also breaks down research-backed insights on overeating, appetite regulation, and how the body responds when protein intake is low, along with"

transcript: In the relationships, after a fight, he's like, "Can we fix this? Tell me what you want me to do." And she's like, "Fix what? I'm still hurt. You're acting like nothing has happened." That's not just attitude, it's different emotion regulation style. A lot of men cope by trying to solve the problem because a solution calms their brain. It makes them feel like the fight is over. But a lot of women need to feel understood because for their brain, repair is him understanding why something hurts. So, when he jumps to solutions, she feels like he's trying to move on instead of realizing his mistake. And when she stays in the hurt, he sees it as punishment. Like nothing he does will ever be enough for her. The fix is actually pretty simple. He needs to stop rushing to solutions, let her feel, talk about it, vent it out. And she needs to ask for solutions after feeling understood and not assume that he can read her mind because it's not about ending the fight as soon as possible. It's about making the other person feel loved again.

full_text_english: Comment ‘500’ and I’ll personally DM you the full conversation.Have you ever had a close encounter with wildlife?In today’s episode, our guest is @paulrosolie, American conservationist, to talk about life inside the jungle and the reality of wildlife conservation.Here’s what we discussed:-Life Inside the Jungle: Experiences from the Amazon rainforest and Western Ghats.-Animal Behavior Decoded: Why snakes avoid humans and moments of unexpected animal intelligence.-India vs Amazon: Why Indian forests can feel more unpredictable and human-influenced.-Illegal Wildlife Trade: One of the world’s biggest black markets and the demand behind it.-Ecosystem Importance: From elephants to ants, how every species plays a critical role.Catch the full episode here:YouTube Channel: Raj ShamaniSpotify Channel: Raj Shamani Figuring OutUntil then, keep figuring out! 🚀Disclaimer - No animals were harmed during the filming of this content. All animals were handled by trained professionals in a controlled environment. This was filmed in New York City, USA.#rajshamani #figuringout

willpower isn’t unlimited, it’s a resource that gets depleted.That’s why systems and habits matter more than relying on motivation.Start your day with hard tasks

Interesting take but I see decision-making, willpower and discipline as different things.Decision-making and willpower can definitely get fatigued over the day. But discipline when built through systems, actually reduces the need for both. Disciplined people aren’t relying on willpower all day and need to make fewer decisions in the first place.
The hack which worked for me, is to make it frictionless to take action as much as possibleThat way I don't depend on motivation alone.

Very true. This is why serious work should be done when the mind is still fresh, not pushed to the leftover hours of the day. Discipline matters, but good leaders also know that performance is shaped by how wisely energy is managed.
How you see something shapes how you respond to it. The same reality can lead to very different outcomes depending on the perspective applied. That difference makes all the impact.

Old productivity:* Rely on discipline* Work long hours* Push through fatigueSmarter approach:* Design your environment* Work in energy cycles* Remove unnecessary decisionsDiscipline fades. Systems don’t.
Makes senseWhich is why relying on willpower is a losing gamesystems carry you when discipline dropsThe real shift is designing your dayso fewer decisions even need to be made

**“When I travel, I often listen to music and visualize the version of myself I aspire to become.In those moments, I don’t just imagine it—I step into that mindset and start embodying it. Over time, that imagination begins to shape my reality, as if I’ve always been meant for it.I’d love to hear your perspective—what does your process look like in detail?”**

Recent research actually suggests this fatigue is linked to the buildup of glutamate in the prefrontal cortex after intense cognitive work. It’s essentially the brain’s way of signaling that it needs a 'system flush' through rest or low-effort tasks to restore balance. Discipline isn't just about grit; it's about neurochemical management.
Makes sense — but it’s not just prefrontal cortex fatigue.Discipline usually drops when energy, routines, and environment aren’t managed well.System strong ho to willpower ki need hi kam padti hai
Discipline doesn’t fade, it gets exposed. If it depends on energy, it was never real

hook_text_english: Adults are losing friends, and it's benefiting the economy.
full_text_english: Adults are losing friends, and it's benefiting the economy.At some point in adulthood, maintaining friendships stopped being automatic and started requiring effort nobody had prepared for. And most people let their friendships fade without ever consciously knowing what was happening.Friendship became collateral damage to everything else.The career move to a different city, the longer hours, the weekends swallowed by errands and family and the general business of being an adult. Friendship produced nothing measurable, nothing that moved anything forward, so it kept getting pushed to the end of the priority list.And in that space, in the gap that was created, an entire economy was built. Everything that friendship used to give people for free is now a product. A social wellness market worth $18.7 billion exists specifically to address loneliness, and it didn't even meaningfully exist a decade ago.To know more about how we reached this position, read here: https://lnkd.in/dcs67SxWhashtag#rajshamani hashtag#figuringout
body_summary_english: A thought-provoking post exploring why adult friendships fade due to career demands, time constraints, and lack of measurable value in a productivity-driven world.
comments: Raj Decision fatigue is real, but it’s not just time-based. Sleep, stress and habits matter. Build systems and routines so discipline doesn’t rely only on willpower.

willpower isn’t unlimited, it’s a resource that gets depleted.That’s why systems and habits matter more than relying on motivation.Start your day with hard tasks
