But discipline when built through systems, actually reduces the need for both. Disciplined people aren’t relying on willpower all day and need to make fewer decisions in the first place.
The hack which worked for me, is to make it frictionless to take action as much as possibleThat way I don't depend on motivation alone.

Very true. This is why serious work should be done when the mind is still fresh, not pushed to the leftover hours of the day. Discipline matters, but good leaders also know that performance is shaped by how wisely energy is managed.
How you see something shapes how you respond to it. The same reality can lead to very different outcomes depending on the perspective applied. That difference makes all the impact.

Old productivity:* Rely on discipline* Work long hours* Push through fatigueSmarter approach:* Design your environment* Work in energy cycles* Remove unnecessary decisionsDiscipline fades. Systems don’t.
Makes senseWhich is why relying on willpower is a losing gamesystems carry you when discipline dropsThe real shift is designing your dayso fewer decisions even need to be made

**“When I travel, I often listen to music and visualize the version of myself I aspire to become.In those moments, I don’t just imagine it—I step into that mindset and start embodying it. Over time, that imagination begins to shape my reality, as if I’ve always been meant for it.I’d love to hear your perspective—what does your process look like in detail?”**

Recent research actually suggests this fatigue is linked to the buildup of glutamate in the prefrontal cortex after intense cognitive work. It’s essentially the brain’s way of signaling that it needs a 'system flush' through rest or low-effort tasks to restore balance. Discipline isn't just about grit; it's about neurochemical management.
Makes sense — but it’s not just prefrontal cortex fatigue.Discipline usually drops when energy, routines, and environment aren’t managed well.System strong ho to willpower ki need hi kam padti hai
Discipline doesn’t fade, it gets exposed. If it depends on energy, it was never real

Interesting point, but this is a bit of an oversimplification.Classic “ego depletion” idea — that willpower is a finite resource that gets used up. But newer research in cognitive science shows it’s not that straightforward.→ The prefrontal cortex doesn’t just “shut down” after 2–3 hours → Fatigue is influenced by motivation, task switching, sleep, glucose levels, and stress → In many studies, perceived fatigue changes based on beliefs and incentives, not just biology So what feels like “discipline disappearing” is often:→ decision fatigue + cognitive overload → not actual depletion of willpower A more accurate framing would be:Your brain doesn’t run out of discipline - it reallocates effort based on perceived cost vs reward.That’s why structure > discipline:→ reduce decisions → automate routines → batch deep work Less about “willpower dying,” more about systems failing under load.

Interesting point—but if discipline is just a limited resource that ‘runs out,’ then are we overvaluing motivation and undervaluing systems? The real question is: are we designing our lives in a way that reduces the need for constant willpower, or are we relying on bursts of discipline and then blaming our biology when we fall off?

This is why success is less about motivation and more about energy management.Most people judge themselves for lacking discipline at night, when the real issue is they spent their best cognitive hours on low-value decisions, distractions, or reactive work.Many top creators and founders do their hardest work early — not because mornings are magical, but because they protect peak mental bandwidth for what matters most.It’s not that you’re lazy in the evening. You’re often just mentally taxed.Bonus tip:Schedule your top 1 task in the first 2–3 hours of the day, automate small decisions (clothes, meals, routines), and use evenings for lighter work or recovery. Discipline grows when friction drops.

It really highlights why discipline isn’t just about willpower, but also about managing energy. Building systems and habits can help stay consistent even when mental fatigue kicks in by the end of the day.

This is a core pillar of survival biology.When the PFC tires out, the amygdala and the limbic system take the wheel, pushing us toward instant gratification and 'safe' familiar habits.Understanding this biological limit is the first step in designing a day that works with our neurochemistry rather than against it.

Interesting point. But it’s less about the brain ‘getting tired’ and more about how we allocate cognitive resources. Discipline isn’t just willpower, it’s systems, habits, and environment design that reduce decision fatigue.”

Makes sense.I’ve noticed this too - by evening it’s not that discipline disappears, the brain just feels tired and starts going for the easiest option.Most bad decisions aren’t because we don’t know better… it’s just that we don’t have the energy left to act on it.
Discipline isn’t just willpower—it’s energy management. Align your toughest tasks with your peak focus hours.

Raj Shamani So true. I’ve found that reducing 'micro-decisions' (like what to wear or what’s for lunch) helps preserve that prefrontal cortex energy for the work that actually moves the needle. Discipline isn't just about grit; it's about managing your biological bandwidth.

This is why I do my deep work before 11 AM now.Used to grind till late evening, thinking I was being productive, was really just burning willpower on easy tasks when my brain was fried.Flipped the schedule after leaving my 9-to-5. Creative work in the morning, admin/calls post lunch. Output doubled.
That's why it is said that taking a short break every 2-3 hours is better than continuosly going at it for 9 hours straight.

We were told about this in our Psychology class, the more decisions they take early in the day, the faster that mental energy gets drained. That’s why pre-deciding small things like what to work on or even what to eat makes a real difference Raj Shamani

I think it is less about the brain getting tired..and more about how we use it.If discipline disappears by evening, it usually means the day was filled with too many unnecessary decisions. The real problem is not willpower..it is lack of systems.
This is why morning routines and deep work blocks matter. Our willpower is a finite resource, best used intentionally and protected from trivial decisions.
So the real productivity hack isn't waking up at 5am — it's protecting those first 2-3 hours like your life depends on it?Most people waste peak brain hours on emails & scrolling. 👀
We blame character when we should be blaming the clock. Most bad decisions aren't weak moments; they're just late ones.
Makes sense why mornings feel so powerful and evenings feel like a test.Real discipline is designing your day so you don’t have to rely on it all the time.
Powerful reminder. Discipline is not just mindset it is energy management. High performers protect their peak hours for deep work and use systems when willpower naturally declines.
Discipline is a finite resource. Spend it wisely on building operations that last.
Raj Shamani Not sure about the exact “2–3 hours” part… but yeah, I do feel my focus dropping after working for a while. Even simple stuff feels harder later in the day.

It’s not just about friendship. I haven’t had friends for a long time. When you are with people, you give your 100%, but they don’t value it, and eventually it starts to feel like your effort had no worth. You keep trying, even while managing your own commitments, but in the end, you realize you were only fooling yourself.It takes years to build a relationship or friendship, but only seconds to break it. Either have friends who are genuine and have good intentions for you, or protect your peace by stepping back from relationships that constantly bring guilt and drama that you didn’t create.
Sumaya Qadir Buland We monetized what used to be free. Loneliness is now a $18B industry. Wild.

A sharp observation. As relationships became harder to maintain, markets stepped in to fill the emotional gaps. But connection was never meant to be outsourced. The real question isn’t just about a growing industry—it’s about what we’re deprioritising. Are we investing enough time in relationships before replacing them with solutions?

---------------

In today’s episode, we sit down with Paul Rosolie, American conservationist, to talk about life inside the jungle and the reality of wildlife conservation.

He shares how he built Junglekeepers, his experiences in the Western Ghats and the Amazon, and why India’s jungles can feel more unpredictable. We discuss how animals actually think, why snakes avoid humans, and moments where animals showed unexpected behavior, from elephant kindness to close encounters with massive snakes.

The conversation also covers the illegal wildlife trade, one of the largest black markets in the world, and the real demand driving it. Paul explains the risks of protecting forests, threats he has faced, and the importance of ecosystems, from large predators to small species like ants. 

Subscribe for more such conversations.

---------------

In today’s episode, we sit down with Paul Rosolie, American conservationist, to talk about life inside the jungle and the reality of wildlife conservation.

He shares how he built Junglekeepers, his experiences in the Western Ghats and the Amazon, and why India’s jungles can feel more unpredictable. We discuss how animals actually think, why snakes avoid humans, and moments where animals showed unexpected behavior, from elephant kindness to close encounters with massive snakes.

The conversation also covers the illegal wildlife trade, one of the largest black markets in the world, and the real demand driving it. Paul explains the risks of protecting forests, threats he has faced, and the importance of ecosystems, from large predators to small species like ants. 

Subscribe for more such conversations.

hook_text_english: What if you could get all AI models to debate each other for the best answer?
full_text_english: Everyone is confused about which AI model to use. But what if you could get all of them to debate each other and give you the best answer?In our recent episode, I talked to Vaibhav Sisinty, Founder of GrowthSchool, about what using AI at full capacity can do.Most people pick one model and hope for the best. GPT, Claude or Gemini. But there is a feature called Model Council, where you can run GPT 5.4, Claude and Gemini simultaneously. Each one researches your problem independently. Then all three debate each other. They challenge each other's answers. They find gaps the other missed. And then they give you one final answer.Three of the world's best AI minds working on your problem at the same time.Vaibhav also showed how to use AI to build an entire business from scratch, step by step, live, on camera. By the end, you'll realise that work that used to take a team of 10 can now be done by one person in minutes. We went from zero to a working idea, so you can see exactly how things are made in the AI world.The opportunity to make money with AI has never been clearer. Full episode out now, watch it here: https://bit.ly/4e1yOhahashtag#rajshamani hashtag#figuringout
body_summary_english: A podcast episode discussing Model Council feature with GrowthSchool founder, showing how running multiple AI models simultaneously creates debate and better solutions.
comments: This is honestly a game-changer perspective.Most people (including me earlier) stick to one AI model and try to make it work, but the idea of a “Model Council” where multiple AIs challenge each other feels like the next level of problem-solving.Also, the part about building a business end-to-end using AI really shows how the barrier to entry is dropping fast. It’s less about resources now and more about how creatively and effectively you use these tools.Feels like we’re at a point where individuals can unlock opportunities that earlier needed full teams. Excited to explore this deeper and apply it in real projects.Great insights as always 🙌
Riya Jain That's true
I’m intrigued by the Model Council feature you mentioned, where GPT, Claude, and Gemini debate each other. Did you and Vaibhav find that one model consistently spots gaps the others miss?

💡 I am Enlighted by :↳ The idea of AI models debating to deliver a sharper, consensus-driven answer↳ How three distinct AI “minds” can catch blind spots in each other’s logic↳ The potential of building a full business solo with AI in minutes instead of teamsBut I am confused about how these debates handle conflicting solutions does the final answer weigh accuracy or creativity more?
The edge won’t be choosing one model—it’ll be knowing how to orchestrate many into one sharp decision.

T M Musavvir - that’s a sharp way to put it.Most people stop at using multiple models,but the real leverage is in how you combine and refine their outputs into one clear decision.That orchestration layer is where the real advantage sits.
This is a powerful shift—from using one model to orchestrating multiple perspectives. The real advantage isn’t just AI, it’s how well you leverage its collective intelligence.
Interesting idea, I've only ever used one model at a time so this debate approach is new to me. Makes me curious to try it and see if the answers actually improve when they challenge each other.

Powerful shift. The real advantage is no longer using one AI model it’s orchestrating multiple models for sharper decisions, faster execution, and better outcomes. The future belongs to people who know how to combine AI with business thinking and execution speed, where small teams can now create outsized impact.

Really insightful, what stood out is not just the tools but how you are thinking about using them. When used the right way AI becomes a thinking partner not just a shortcut. This kind of clarity is what most people are missing.

Aditya Dash — well said.The shift from using AI as a shortcutto using it as a thinking partneris probably the biggest mindset gap right now.Most people are speeding up execution…but not upgrading their thinking.That’s where the real edge is.

The real power of AI isn’t choosing one tool—it’s learning how to think with many. When different models challenge each other, you don’t just get answers, you get sharper thinking. The edge now belongs to those who know how to orchestrate intelligence, not just consume it. That’s where real leverage begins.
This kind of changes what “expertise” means. like its less about knowing answers, and more about designing thinking processes.
Interesting concept. Using multiple models to challenge each other can lead to deeper and more reliable insights.This kind of approach really shows how powerful AI can be when used beyond the basics.

Kinjan Shah - that’s a sharp question.From what I’ve seen, it’s rarely about one model being consistently better.Each one tends to catch different blind spots based on how the problem is framed.The real advantage seems to come fromhow well you can orchestrate their strengthsrather than expecting one to outperform the rest.That’s where most people miss the edge.

commentsTranslated.8: Powerful shift. The real advantage is no longer using one AI model it's orchestrating multiple models for sharper decisions, faster execution, and better outcomes. The future belongs to people who know how to combine AI with business thinking and execution speed, where small teams can now create outsized impact.
commentsTranslated.10: Aditya Dash — well said.The shift from using AI as a shortcutto using it as a thinking partneris probably the biggest mindset gap right now.Most people are speeding up execution…but not upgrading their thinking.That's where the real edge is.
commentsTranslated.11: The real power of AI isn't choosing one tool—it's learning how to think with many. When different models challenge each other, you don't just get answers, you get sharper thinking. The edge now belongs to those who know how to orchestrate intelligence, not just consume it. That's where real leverage begins.
commentsTranslated.12: This kind of changes what "expertise" means. like its less about knowing answers, and more about designing thinking processes.
commentsTranslated.14: Kinjan Shah - that's a sharp question.From what I've seen, it's rarely about one model being consistently better.Each one tends to catch different blind spots based on how the problem is framed.The real advantage seems to come fromhow well you can orchestrate their strengthsrather than expecting one to outperform the rest.That's where most people miss the edge.

hook_text_english: Your brain stops wanting things you can't get—here's the psychological reason why.
full_text_english: You know how sometimes you really want something. It could be a role, a person, or an opportunity. And when you do not get it, it hurts for a bit. But then slowly, without even trying, you stop wanting it. You tell yourself it wasn't really right for you anyway, and you genuinely believe it.This is not you being strong or moving on, but your brain doing something specific to relieve you of the pain of not getting what you wanted.There is a well-documented reason why your brain does that.Check the doc below to know what is happening. hashtag#rajshamani hashtag#figuringout
body_summary_english: A post explaining the psychological mechanism behind how the brain relieves pain from unmet desires, offering a linked resource for deeper understanding.
comments: It’s fascinating how the mind protects us from disappointment by quietly changing the narrative. What feels like acceptance is often a coping mechanism to reduce the discomfort of rejection.But recognising this pattern is important—because sometimes we stop wanting something not because it wasn’t right, but because it hurt too much to keep wanting it. That awareness is where real clarity begins.
Neeru Yadav Hockey Wali Sarpanch correct

What feels like “moving on” is often your mind protecting itself from disappointment. We don’t always stop wanting things—we reshape the story to ease the loss. It’s a quiet survival mechanism. Recognizing it doesn’t make you weak; it makes you aware, and awareness is where real emotional strength begins.

There’s a real psychological pattern behind what you’re describing, often linked to how the brain reduces emotional discomfort after rejection or disappointment.When something we strongly want doesn’t happen, the mind can gradually reframe its value so the mismatch feels less painful. It’s less about “moving on logically” and more about emotional regulation happening in the background—your perception adjusts to restore balance. Raj Shamani

We don’t always “move on.”We just rewrite the story to hurt less.The mind protects before it accepts.But there’s a difference between healingand quietly lowering your standards.Sometimes it wasn’t meant for you.Sometimes you just stopped fighting for it.Clarity comes when you askdid I outgrow it… or did I give up on it?

This is a fascinating breakdown of our Mental Defense System, Raj Shamani! It’s a relief to know that Cognitive Dissonance Reduction is a biological protection mechanism. Understanding this helps us stay objective during career transitions rather than letting the brain rewrite our history just to avoid a little sting.

Interesting how this mechanism is often interpreted as maturity,when in reality it is a form of preservation.The more subtle point is that we don’t always stop wanting something because it wasn’t rightbut because continuing to want it becomes emotionally expensive.So the real question isn’t just about “moving on”but whether there was clarity or simply adaptation.Because better decisions come from awarenessnot just relief.

This is such an underrated behavioural pattern. Sometimes we do not move on logically, we simply rewrite the story to reduce the pain of wanting something we could not get. This makes cognitive dissonance feel very human, not just psychological.

Fascinating insight—and it connects directly to something we explored earlier: the illusion of free will.Your brain rewires wanting to protect you from pain. But here's the deeper layer: that rewiring isn't "you" deciding to move on. It's your environment shaping your neural pathways without your conscious permission.We are not independent agents. We are products of our surroundings—the people, the culture, the daily inputs. An old saying holds true: "It takes a village to raise a child." But it also takes a village to shape every adult's desires, attachments, and even their ability to let go.Your behavior, your "choices," your shifting wants—all reflections of the environment you live in. Once you see that, you stop blaming yourself for wanting or not wanting. And you start designing your environment deliberately.That's the only real freedom.
Want fades when the brain rewires desire into memory. That's the quiet adaptation that turns loss into forward momentum.

This doesn’t just apply to roles or people. It shows up in conversions too. When interest doesn’t turn into action, it’s easy to assume “they weren’t serious,” instead of looking at what in the process made it easy to drop off.

Cognitive dissonance is often invisible, but it quietly shapes most of our decisions.We don’t always change our beliefs when reality contradicts them, we adjust the story instead, just to reduce discomfort.The real shift happens when you stop defending your decisions and start examining them honestly. That’s where clarity begins.Great post, this is one of those concepts that explains more of human behavior than we like to admit.

This sounds a lot like cognitive dissonance in action—aligning our beliefs with outcomes to reduce internal discomfort. Powerful, but also something to be aware of when making long-term decisions. Not detachment. Just the brain choosing peace over conflict.

Once you see that, you stop blaming yourself for wanting or not wanting. And you start designing your environment deliberately.That's the only real freedom.
commentsTranslated.10: This doesn't just apply to roles or people. It shows up in conversions too. When interest doesn't turn into action, it's easy to assume "they weren't serious," instead of looking at what in the process made it easy to drop off.
commentsTranslated.11: Cognitive dissonance is often invisible, but it quietly shapes most of our decisions.We don't always change our beliefs when reality contradicts them, we adjust the story instead, just to reduce discomfort.The real shift happens when you stop defending your decisions and start examining them honestly. That's where clarity begins.Great post, this is one of those concepts that explains more of human behavior than we like to admit.
commentsTranslated.12: This sounds a lot like cognitive dissonance in action—aligning our beliefs with outcomes to reduce internal discomfort. Powerful, but also something to be aware of when making long-term decisions. Not detachment. Just the brain choosing peace over conflict.

Man this was insane 😭

I know a lot of people will say, “if AI can make crores, why not just build instead of talking about it?” Fair question. But the truth is, we ARE building. Both of us. I’m running a 200+ team across 4 brands, and Raj is among the top creators globally, curaa is absolutely killing it.

So instead of looking at this negatively, look at it as, how do I get an unfair advantage from this?

What you’re seeing in this podcast is a very raw, honest download of how both of us think. How we break problems, how we look at opportunities, what we choose to do and what we consciously avoid. This is literally us debating in real time.

But there’s also a reality here…

Millions of people will watch this.
A lot will start something.
Some will drop off early.
And a small % will stay consistent and actually push through, those are the people who end up winning.

This podcast is for anyone who genuinely wants to build.

If that’s you, go all in. Don’t just watch this passively. Pause, take notes, act on ideas. The goal isn’t to consume, it’s to create.

This is something both Raj and I deeply believe in, that India can lead in execution. We can own the application layer. This is our small attempt towards that vision.

All the best, go build, go win 💪

And let us know what you want us to cover next, we’re planning to do this every 30 days 👍

Hi Raj Shamani,

This content is entirely in Hindi, but it contains a lot of valuable knowledge, thanks to Vaibhav Sisinty, and it explains how to use artificial intelligence effectively. However, it is only useful for those who understand Hindi.

Your audience is not limited to Hindi speakers. There are Tamil speakers, Telugu speakers, Malayalis, and many others who speak different languages or are multilingual. If this content were provided in English, it would be accessible to a much wider audience.

English acts as a common language in India, and many people can understand it, even if they do not know Hindi. By providing the content in English, it would become more useful and inclusive for everyone.

So, why not consider creating this content fully in English? Please consider this.

transcript: सैम ऑल्टमैन इज सेइंग दैट एक इंसान ना मिलियन डॉलर कंपनी बना देगा। हाउ अगर सिंगल पर्सन कंपनी बनाना है तो फर्स्ट जो प्रोडक्ट मैं बना रहा हूं उसके लिए डिमांड है कि नहीं? टू क्या वो यूजर एक्विजिशन जो है वो प्रोडक्ट का डिजिटली होता है कि फिजिकली होता है डिजिटल प्रोडक्ट्स के लिए ये अच्छे से काम करेगा। थ्री प्रोडक्ट का प्राइस ऐसे होना चाहिए जिसके लिए लोग ज्यादा नहीं सोचेंगे खरीदने के लिए। एंड रियलिस्टिकली कितने पैसे बना सकते हैं लोग? अनलिमिटेड। आई थिंक डेटिंग एंड दिस कोच विल बी क्रेजी। शुड वी बिल्ड दैट? आई मीन लाइक राइट नाउ ऑन द कॉल। हां, ठीक है। हाय। सिल्क और आपका नाम? मेरा नाम वैभव है। वैभव अच्छा नाम है। वैसे क्या कर रहे हो आप काम को? हर महीने करोड़ों रुपए कैसे कमा? यूज़ समथिंग लाइक जूलियस एi। यू कैन कनेक्ट योर शॉपिफाई। जो भी सर्विस यूज़ करते हो। यू कैन जस्ट कम एंड से हाउ इज माई परफॉरमेंस डूइंग। एंड विल जस्ट टेल यू यू यू यू यू यू यू लाइक अ डाटा एनालिस्ट। फॉर कस्टमर सक्सेस। यू यूजिंग वापी। फॉर रनिंग एड्स यू यूजिंग मैजिक्स। फॉर क्रिएटिंग एड्स यू आर यूजिंग हिग्स फील्ड। फॉर राइटिंग कोड, यू आर यूजिंग समथिंग लाइक अ रेप्लेट इमरजेंट। देर इस स्टडी अराउंड 80% टू 85% ऑफ़ द पीपल यूज़ एआई। लेकिन 2 टू 3% आर एबल टू यूज़ एI इफेक्टिवली द वेट शुड। हैव यू हर्ड ऑफ़ क्लॉडमिक्स? नहीं। मार्क ज़करबर्ग मेटा के अंदर एक कॉम्पटीशन चला रहा है विथ अ लीडर बोर्ड। टू शो हू आर द हाईएस्ट यूज़र्स ऑफ़ एआई। पूरा एंप्लाइजज़। एवरीबॉडी हैज़ अनलिमिटेड क्रेडिट्स ऑफ़ क्लॉट। एक बंदे ने 2.8 बिलियन टोकंस यूज़ किया है। इन वन मंथ वैल्यू ऑफ़ द टोकन इज़ $1.4 मिलियन। तू सोच रहा होगा भाई कितना क्वेश्चन पूछ रहा है वो? क्वेश्चन नहीं पूछ रहा है वो। ही इज़ रनिंग मल्टीपल एआई एजेंट्स पैरेलली वर्किंग फॉर हिम। तो व्हेन पीपल से जॉब्स ये सब चला जाएगा। एक्चुअली जॉब्स जा नहीं रहा है। जॉब्स इवॉल्व हो रहा है। द प्रॉब्लम इज आपका जॉब इसीलिए जा रहा है क्योंकि आप खुद इवॉल्व नहीं हो रहे हो। वाइल्ड। मेरा दिमाग घूम रहा है अभी। एंड आई जस्ट लव दिस। यह हमारा नेक्स्ट एपिसोड है फिगरिंग आउट एआई का विद वैभव। दिस एपिसोड ब्लू माय माइंड। क्योंकि यह एपिसोड खत्म होते-होते आपको पता चलेगा शायद मैं मेरा काम जो 10 लोग मेरी टीम में करते थे, वो मैं अकेला कर सकता हूं इन मिनट्स। एंड दी ओपोरर्चुनिटी टू मेक मनी आई थिंक इस पॉडकास्ट के बाद बहुत क्लियर हो गई है। सो प्लीज वॉच इट। एंड अगर आपको और बहुत सारे एआई टूल्स के बारे में सीखना है क्योंकि एआई से बहुत पैसे बनने वाले हैं। बहुत लोग बहुत पैसे बना रहे हैं। बहुत करियर में आगे जा रहे हैं। अपना काम बेटर कर रहे हैं। अपनी प्रोडक्टिविटी बेटर करिए अगर आपको भी यह करना है। सो वैभव एंड मी वी हैव स्टार्टेड अ कम्युनिटी फिगरिंग आउट एआई जिसकी लिंक डिस्क्रिप्शन में है। और इस बार हमने एक नया फॉर्मेट ट्राई किया है जहां पे वैभव मुझे लाइफ सिखाता है। एंड मेरे लिए चीजें करता है। सो दैट ईच एंड एव्री वन ऑफ यू आप पेन एंड पेपर ले के बैठो और आपको भी स्टेप बाय स्टेप देखने को मिलेगा कि कैसे जीरो से एक चीज बिल्ड की जाती है एआई के वर्ल्ड में। सो दिस एपिसोड इज नॉट क्वेश्चन एंड आंसर। यह एपिसोड है कि कैसे जीरो से चीजें बनाते हैं। दिस इज बिल्ड इन पब्लिक। हमने पूरा एक बिजनेस बनाया है फ्रॉम जीरो बाय द हेल्प ऑफ एआई। एंड अगर आपको अच्छा आईडिया लगे वो तो प्लीज चुरा लेना। वेलकम बैक वैभव सिस इट्स फनी दैट मोस्ट पीपल विल नॉट नो व्हाई दे आर लाफिंग यू शुड टेल देम तेरे को बताना चाहिए क्यों हंस रहा है तो 5 घंटे से पिछले 5 घंटे से मैं यहीं बैठा हुआ है एंड 5 घंटे से हम लोग बात कर रहे हैं एंड उसने मुझे टेक्निकली अभी सिखाया कि कैसे मैं मेरे पॉडकास्ट की रिसर्च 10 एक्स बेटर कर सकता हूं विद जस्ट फर्स्ट एआई एंड जो छह सात लोग मिलके काम करते हैं वो मैं एक घंटे में कैसे करवा सकता हूं एआई से और वो पूरी स्किल बना के उसने मुझे सिखाई है और बनाई है वो इस पॉडकास्ट के एंड सेक्शन में है। सो इफ एनीबडी वांट्स टू वॉच दैट कंप्लीटली वांट्स टू अंडरस्टैंड हाउ टू टेक माई पॉडकास्ट माई मेकिंग माय पडकास्ट बेटर एंड आई एम गोंना यूज़ डेट प्रोसेस। मैं वही यूज़ करने वाला हूं। सो यू कैन स्टील माई फ्रेमवर्क ऑफ मेकिंग पॉडकास्ट बेटर व्हिच वैभव हास टॉट मी टुडे। दैट वाज जस्ट 2 आवर्स आउट ऑफ़ फाइव आवर्स। द नेक्स्ट 3 आवर्स वाज टू टू टू बॉयज टॉकिंग फ्रेंड्स सिटींग टुगेदर एंड टॉकिंग। दैट्स ऑलवेज द केस विथ यू। सो, बाई द वे, यह पॉडकास्ट और यह कॉन्वर्सेशन मैं वैभव के साथ हर महीने इसीलिए करता हूं ताकि मैं एआई वर्ल्ड में क्या-क्या चल रहा है, वो सीखते जाऊं। आई थिंक आई नीड टू डू दिस मोर। मतलब एक

commentsTranslated.0: Every episode, we try to leave you with something valuable. If this one did that for you, make sure you subscribe to the channel. See you in the next one. 🫡 ~ RS
commentsTranslated.3: Finally a podcast that shows AI doing actual work instead of just talking about it 🙌 demo segment was chef's kiss. More pls.
commentsTranslated.4:
Man this was insane 😭

I know a lot of people will say, "if AI can make crores, why not just build instead of talking about it?" Fair question. But the truth is, we ARE building. Both of us. I'm running a 200+ team across 4 brands, and Raj is among the top creators globally, curaa is absolutely killing it.

So instead of looking at this negatively, look at it as, how do I get an unfair advantage from this?

What you're seeing in this podcast is a very raw, honest download of how both of us think. How we break problems, how we look at opportunities, what we choose to do and what we consciously avoid. This is literally us debating in real time.

But there's also a reality here…

Millions of people will watch this.
A lot will start something.
Some will drop off early.
And a small % will stay consistent and actually push through, those are the people who end up winning.

This podcast is for anyone who genuinely wants to build.

If that's you, go all in. Don't just watch this passively. Pa

transcript: If you want to break out the generational curse in the family and go win really, really big, then you need to be really fully delusional, unapologetic, restless, and the most confident about what you're capable of. No one's going to be cheering for you harder than you. You need to become your biggest hype man, your own hype woman, your own PR machine, your own fan army. Clap for yourself the loudest when nobody's clapping and pick yourself up again and again. And if you keep doing this for a very long time with enough proof of work, eventually others will come along and start cheering for you. But until that happens, you are your first and the only loudest believer.

hook_text_english: What happens when you put India's boldest voices in a room with the sharpest minds?
full_text_english: What happens when you put India’s boldest voices in a room with the sharpest minds on the planet?That’s exactly what we set out to do. We took Figuring Out across the US to sit down with the people who are rewriting the rules, the thinkers, builders, and disruptors who operate at a level most never get access to.And none of this would be possible without the right travel partner.@goibibo made this entire US tour happen from sorting our flights, hotels, and everything in between so we could focus on what matters most: bringing you conversations that actually change how you think.When you’re moving city to city, guest to guest, on a packed schedule, you need a platform that keeps up with your pace. That’s exactly what Goibibo does.And if you haven’t tried it yet, there are some great first-time discounts waiting for you.#ad
body_summary_english: Figuring Out took their show across the US to interview thought leaders and disruptors. Goibibo sponsored the tour by handling all travel logistics, enabling the podcast to focus on quality conversations.

hook_text_english: The biggest distribution advantage today is giving away value free.
full_text_english: The biggest distribution advantage today is doing the thing nobody wants to do: giving away value for free.Today, when people start building distribution, their first instinct is to gatekeep their genuinely valuable stuff. Put it behind a paywall, save it for a course, hold it for the people who pay. It makes logical sense. Why would you give away the thing that makes you money?But distribution follows generosity. It always has.The people who have built huge, trustworthy distribution gave away so much value for so long that by the time they finally monetised, the audience already trusted them completely. The decision to pay was a formality. The relationship was already built long before the purchase decision ever showed up.When you hold your best stuff back, you are choosing short-term revenue over long-term influence. And in a world where trust is the hardest thing to earn, that trade almost never works in your favour.Distribution is just an unreasonable amount of value, given freely, long enough that people start trusting you and also start telling other people about it.hashtag#rajshamani hashtag#figuringout
body_summary_english: Argues that generous free content builds audience trust faster than paywalls; distribution follows generosity, enabling future monetization.
comments: Most people don’t gatekeep because they’re strategic — they gatekeep because they’re unsure their value can stand in the open.If giving it away for free feels risky, it usually means you don’t fully trust what you’re offering yet.
Sagar Kapoor "Distribution follows generosity." This is gold. Give first. Win later.

Holding back value feels safe, but it usually slows down the very trust you’re trying to monetize later.The people who win here aren’t just generous—they’re consistent enough that the market stops questioning their depth. Raj Shamani
Swatilekha Das true. Trust is built before the sale even exists. Stop gatekeeping.

Real leverage isn’t built by chasing attention—it’s earned through consistent generosity. When you give meaningful value without expecting immediate returns, trust compounds quietly. Over time, people don’t just follow you, they believe in you—and that belief is what truly scales, far beyond any strategy or shortcut.
There’s a saying: “You can't pour from an empty cup.” Giving away value freely builds trust, and that trust fills your cup over time. It’s a slow, steady way to create something real and lasting.

This is such an important reminder. A lot of people try to protect their “best knowledge” thinking that’s what gives them an edge.  But in reality, holding back slows down trust more than it builds value. I’ve started realising that the more clearly you share what you know, the more people begin to see how you think — and that’s what actually builds credibility. Information can be copied. But clarity and consistency can’t.And that’s what eventually compounds into trust.

Nothing is wrong… and that’s exactly the problem. Comfort doesn’t look dangerous… but it quietly kills momentum. What’s one thing you know you should change but haven’t yet? 👇 hashtag#GrowthMindset hashtag#ComfortZone hashtag#SelfAwareness hashtag#Action
Free value isn’t giving away your business… it’s proving you’re worth paying for. Most people don’t lose revenue by sharing too much, they lose trust by sharing too little.
Holding back the best insights may feel safe, but sharing them openly is what actually builds long‑term influence.

Insightful perspective, sir. In today’s attention economy, trust truly compounds faster than revenue. Giving away real, actionable value not only builds credibility but also lowers the barrier for meaningful engagement. Over time, it shifts the dynamic from “selling” to “serving,” where conversions become a natural outcome of trust rather than persuasion. A great reminder that sustainable distribution is less about protecting information and more about consistently showing up with generosity and authenticity.

Raj Shamani In every industry I’ve worked in- including oil & gas- the first win may come from positioning, but the second always comes from performance. You can market a promise once. But you can only scale what consistently delivers. That’s where real businesses are built- quietly, below the surface.

Realising this early—It’s not about knowing everything before you share.It’s about sharing as you learn.People don’t connect with perfection,they connect with progress.That’s where trust actually starts.hashtag#buildinpublic hashtag#learning

Distribution today isn’t built on content alone—it’s built on trust. And trust compounds when value is given consistently, without immediate expectation of return.Holding back might protect short-term revenue, but it often limits long-term reach and influence. The ones who win are those who give enough for people to believe in them before they ever buy from them.

I’ve seen this play out firsthand.The people who win long-term are the ones who: → teach openly→ share transparently→ and delay monetization longer than feels comfortableBy the time they sell, the audience is already sold.Raj Shamani | Hari Baheti | Figuring Out Media & Education

I agree with this a lot. But one nuance I’ve noticed is that free value alone isn’t enough for distribution. A lot of people give away great insights but still don’t get reach because it isn’t packaged or placed where attention already exists. So it’s really a mix. Generosity builds trust, consistency builds familiarity, and distribution happens when both are combined with intentional visibility. That’s where things usually compound.

It feels counterintuitive at first, but generosity usually compounds into distribution over time.The audience builds trust long before monetization happens.What made you personally lean into this approach? Raj Shamani

Giving value for free does build trust and distribution. But not everything should be free. If you give away everything, you risk attracting people who consume but never convert.The smarter play is give enough value to earn trust, but keep depth, structure, or execution as the paid layer. Distribution isn’t just generosity it’s generosity with boundaries.

This is such a powerful mindset shift. Most people think value should be protected, but in reality, it’s generosity that builds trust and reach. When you consistently give without holding back, you don’t just grow an audience—you build belief. And once people trust you, selling isn’t convincing… it’s a natural next step. That’s how real, lasting distribution is created. ✨

People don’t follow value—they follow consistency of value. Giving away your best ideas isn’t losing leverage, it’s building trust at scale. By the time you sell, you’re not convincing, you’re converting. Distribution isn’t about reach first, it’s about trust compounding over time.

Gatekeeping feels safe. Generosity feels like risk. But only one of them scales.  We confuse “value” with “inventory.” You don’t run out of insights by sharing them. You run out of trust by hoarding them.  The irony: the people most scared to give away their “best stuff” don’t realize it only becomes their best stuff _after_ the market validates it. And the market can’t validate what it never sees.  “Distribution is just an unreasonable amount of value, given freely” — that line should be on every founder’s wall. Because attention isn’t bought anymore. It’s earned in installments of free, and the interest is trust.  Well said, Raj. The paywall goes up easy. The moat gets built when people would riot if you stopped posting for free.

Trust builds when value is shared consistently and openly. Offering it freely doesn’t lower your worth, it makes it easier for people to believe in you. When results are visible, the decision to pay feels obvious, not pressured. The real test isn’t tactics, it’s staying patient.

most people confuse protecting value with building leverage, but in distribution, hiding your best work usually slows trust more than it protects revenue.the real compounding happens when people experience your best thinking before they ever have to pay for it.

This flips the usual instinct and rightly so Raj.Most people protect value to monetise it.The few who win distribute value to compound trust.Because in today’s market, attention isn’t scarce, trust is.When you consistently give away insight that actually helps:→ You reduce perceived risk→ You build authority without saying it→ You let results speak before you sellAnd that changes the buying dynamic completely.By the time you monetise, you’re not convincing, you’re converting belief into action.The real trade-off isn’t free vs paid.It’s short-term extraction vs long-term leverage.Give enough value, long enough, and distribution stops being a strategy.It becomes reputation.

Giving away value for free isn't a new concept; it’s ancient wisdom. In India, profound teachings like the Gita have always been available to everyone for free. Yet, the lesson remains: accessibility doesn't guarantee transformation. Many speak about the Gita, but only a few actually apply its value. Free distribution builds the bridge, but the audience still has to do the work to cross it
what worked for us was giving away the “what” for free but keeping the “how to implement step-by-step” paid. people don’t pay for info anymore, they pay for clarity and execution

This hits hard 💯But ek doubt hai… free value dena powerful hai, agreed.Par kya har creator ke liye sustainable hai?Kyuki starting me log itna de dete hain free me ki baad me monetize karna awkward ho jata hai 😅Shayad balance yeh ho sakta hai:free me trust build karo, par clarity bhi ho ki paid me kya deeper milega.Aapke hisaab se line kaha draw karni chahiye - kitna free, kitna paid?

Giving value for free isn’t generosityit’s positioningIf people can learn from youthey start trusting youAnd trust compounds faster than contentThe real leverage is thisbe useful before you try to be profitable

This is exactly how I’ve grown on Upwork and LinkedIn.Most of my inbound came after consistently sharing real strategies and breakdowns for free.Once trust is built, clients don’t hesitate to pay. it becomes a natural next step.

This hits deeper than it looks.Most people don’t struggle with distribution…they struggle with trust.And trust isn’t built when you finally sell —it’s built in the moments where you had the chance to give… and still chose to give.That’s the paradox:The more you hold back to ‘protect value’,the more invisible you become.The ones who win long-term aren’t the smartest…they’re the most consistently generous.What’s one thing you started giving away for free that actually grew your influence?👇 Comment “VALUE” if you believe generosity compounds.

commentsTranslated.10: Insightful perspective, sir. In today's attention economy, trust truly compounds faster than revenue. Giving away real, actionable value not only builds credibility but also lowers the barrier for meaningful engagement. Over time, it shifts the dynamic from "selling" to "serving," where conversions become a natural outcome of trust rather than persuasion. A great reminder that sustainable distribution is less about protecting information and more about consistently showing up with generosity and authenticity.
commentsTranslated.11: Raj Shamani In every industry I've worked in- including oil & gas- the first win may come from positioning, but the second always comes from performance. You can market a promise once. But you can only scale what consistently delivers. That's where real businesses are built- quietly, below the surface.
commentsTranslated.12: Realising this early—It's not about knowing everything before you share.It's about sharing as you learn.People don't connect with perfection,they connect with progress.That's where trust actually starts.hashtag#buildinpublic hashtag#learning
commentsTranslated.13: Distribution today isn't built on content alone—it's built on trust. And trust compounds when value is given consistently, without immediate expectation of return.Holding back might protect short-term revenue, but it often limits long-term reach and influence. The ones who win are those who give enough for people to believe in them before they ever buy from them.
commentsTranslated.14: The hard part is patience. Most people quit before trust compounds because the ROI isn't immediate.
commentsTranslated.16: I've seen this play out firsthand.The people who win long-term are the ones who: → teach openly→ share transparently→ and delay monetization longer than feels comfortableBy the time they sell, the audience is already sold.Raj Shamani | Hari Baheti | Figuring Out Media & Education
commentsTranslated.17: I agree with this a lot. But one nuance I've noticed is that free value alone isn't enough for distribution. A lot of people give away great insights but still don't get reach because it isn't packaged or placed where attention already exists. So it's really a mix. Generosity builds trust, consistency builds familiarity, and distribution happens when both are combined with intentional visibility. That's where things usually compound.
commentsTranslated.19: Giving value for free does build trust and distribution. But not everything should be free. If you give away everything, you risk attracting people who consume but never convert.The smarter play is give enough value to earn trust, but keep depth, structure, or execution as the paid layer. Distribution isn't just generosity it's generosity with boundaries.
commentsTranslated.20: This is such a powerful mindset shift. Most people think value should be protected, but in reality, it's generosity that builds trust and reach. When you consistently give without holding back, you don't just grow an audience—you build belief. And once people trust you, selling isn't convincing… it's a natural next step. That's how real, lasting distribution is created. ✨
commentsTranslated.21: People don't follow value—they follow consistency of value. Giving away your best ideas isn't losing leverage, it's building trust at scale. By the time you sell, you're not convincing, you're converting. Distribution isn't about reach first, it's about trust compounding over time.
commentsTranslated.22: Gatekeeping feels safe. Generosity feels like risk. But only one of them scales.  We confuse "value" with "inventory." You don't run out of insights by sharing them. You run out of trust by hoarding them.  The irony: the people most scared to give away their "best stuff" don't realize it only becomes their best stuff _after_ the market validates it. And the market can't validate what it never sees.  "Distribution is just an unreasonable amount of value, given freely" — that line should be on every founder's wall. Because attention isn't bought anymore. It's earned in installments of free, and the interest is trust.  Well said, Raj. The paywall goes up easy. The moat gets built when people would riot if you stopped posting for free.
commentsTranslated.23: Trust builds when value is shared consistently and openly. Offering it freely doesn't lower your worth, it makes it easier for people to believe in you. When results are visible, the decision to pay feels obvious, not pressured. The real test isn't tactics, it's staying patient.
commentsTranslated.25: This flips the usual instinct and rightly so Raj.Most people protect value to monetise it.The few who win distribute value to compound trust.Because in today's market, attention isn't scarce, trust is.When you consistently give away insight that actually helps:→ You reduce perceived risk→ You build authority without saying it→ You let results speak before you sellAnd that changes the buying dynamic completely.By the time you monetise, you're not convincing, you're converting belief into action.The real trade-off isn't free vs paid.It's short-term extraction vs long-term leverage.Give enough value, long enough, and distribution stops being a strategy.It becomes reputation.
commentsTranslated.26: Giving away value for free isn't a new concept; it's ancient wisdom. In India, profound teachings like the Gita have always been available to everyone for free. Yet, the lesson remains: accessibility doesn't guarantee transformation. Many speak about the Gita, but only a few actually apply its value. Free distribution builds the bridge, but the audience still has to do the work to cross it
commentsTranslated.27: what worked for us was giving away the "what" for free but keeping the "how to implement step-by-step" paid. people don't pay for info anymore, they pay for clarity and execution
commentsTranslated.28: This hits hard 💯But I have one doubt… giving free value is powerful, agreed.But is it sustainable for every creator?Because in the beginning, people give away so much for free that monetizing later becomes awkward 😅Maybe the balance could be:build trust through free content, but also have clarity on what deeper value the paid offering provides.In your opinion, where should the line be drawn — how much free, how much paid?
commentsTranslated.30: Giving value for free isn't generosityit's positioningIf people can learn from youthey start trusting youAnd trust compounds faster than contentThe real leverage is thisbe useful before you try to be profitable
commentsTranslated.31: giving value for free builds trust faster than any ad spend. people remember who taught them something useful before ever asking for anything back — that's how distribution compounds over time
commentsTranslated.32: This is exactly how I've grown on Upwork and LinkedIn.Most of my inbound came after consistently sharing real strategies and breakdowns for free.Once trust is built, clients don't hesitate to pay. it becomes a natural next step.
commentsTranslated.33: This hits deeper than it looks.Most people don't struggle with distribution…they struggle with trust.And trust isn't built when you finally sell —it's built in the moments where you had the chance to give… and still chose to give.That's the paradox:The more you hold back to 'protect value',the more invisible you become.The ones who win long-term aren't the smartest…they're the most consistently generous.What's one thing you started giving away for free that actually grew your influence?👇 Comment "VALUE" if you believe generosity compounds.
commentsTranslated.34: Your mind is not a mirror of reality—it's a product of repetition. Feed it doubt, and it will shrink your world. Feed it belief, and it will expand your limits. Choose your inner dialogue carefully, because what you repeat daily doesn't just reflect your life—it quietly shapes your future.
commentsTranslated.36: True.A year ago, I was jobless, under debt, and constantly hearing doubts from people around me.The real shift didn't come from changing circumstances — it came from changing how I saw my situation.Once that changed, decisions became clearer, and I started building my own path.Nothing overnight, just consistent action.Interesting how things start moving once your thinking aligns.

hook_text_english: Gen Z is not killing traditional wear—they're buying saree styles from their grandmothers
full_text_english: The market assumed that Gen Z would kill traditional wear, but the opposite is happening.They're going back to what their grandmothers wore. 50-60-year-old saree styles are being recreated and sold as vintage. And Gen Z is the one buying.A ₹70,000 crore market with 80% still unorganised is growing because of it.In our recent episode, I talked to Nagakanaka Durga Prasad Chalavadi, founder of SSKL, who built a 1,650-crore saree empire from his basement.He explained that fashion is circular. What felt outdated to one generation becomes heritage for the next. Gen Z is reclaiming culture on their own terms.He started with a single store in Hyderabad in 2005. Today, SSKL runs 81 stores across South India with over ₹1,300 crore in revenue. Competing with established brand names in a market they entered much later.The opportunity in the saree industry is massive but almost entirely unorganised. No reliable sizing, standardised quality, or trust. So he went directly to the weavers and cut out every middleman. The weaver got paid better, and the customer got the best price. There was no bargaining and inflated margins. He built trust over 25 years. Today, 100 people walk into his stores, and 95 of them buy. In retail globally, even 30 to 40% is considered good.He even runs his stores on AI now. If a customer walks in and the staff don't attend to them, it gets flagged. Conversion rates are tracked daily.He has understood his customers and updated systems according to their preferences and time. Full episode out now, watch it here: https://bit.ly/48CrMvChashtag#rajshamani hashtag#figuringout
body_summary_english: Gen Z is driving growth in India's ₹70,000 crore saree market by reclaiming vintage styles. Recent podcast episode features SSKL founder discussing how fashion cycles and cultural heritage intersect.
comments: Most people don’t gatekeep because they’re strategic — they gatekeep because they’re unsure their value can stand in the open.If giving it away for free feels risky, it usually means you don’t fully trust what you’re offering yet.
Sagar Kapoor "Distribution follows generosity." This is gold. Give first. Win later.

Holding back value feels safe, but it usually slows down the very trust you’re trying to monetize later.The people who win here aren’t just generous—they’re consistent enough that the market stops questioning their depth. Raj Shamani
Swatilekha Das true. Trust is built before the sale even exists. Stop gatekeeping.

Real leverage isn’t built by chasing attention—it’s earned through consistent generosity. When you give meaningful value without expecting immediate returns, trust compounds quietly. Over time, people don’t just follow you, they believe in you—and that belief is what truly scales, far beyond any strategy or shortcut.
There’s a saying: “You can't pour from an empty cup.” Giving away value freely builds trust, and that trust fills your cup over time. It’s a slow, steady way to create something real and lasting.

This is such an important reminder. A lot of people try to protect their “best knowledge” thinking that’s what gives them an edge.  But in reality, holding back slows down trust more than it builds value. I’ve started realising that the more clearly you share what you know, the more people begin to see how you think — and that’s what actually builds credibility. Information can be copied. But clarity and consistency can’t.And that’s what eventually compounds into trust.

Nothing is wrong… and that’s exactly the problem. Comfort doesn’t look dangerous… but it quietly kills momentum. What’s one thing you know you should change but haven’t yet? 👇 hashtag#GrowthMindset hashtag#ComfortZone hashtag#SelfAwareness hashtag#Action

A great reminder that sustainable distribution is less about protecting information and more about consistently showing up with generosity and authenticity.

This hits hard 💯But ek doubt hai… free value dena powerful hai, agreed.Par kya har creator ke liye sustainable hai?Kyuki starting me log itna de dete hain free me ki baad me monetize karna awkward ho jata hai 😅Shayad balance yeh ho sakta hai:free me trust build karo, par clarity bhi ho ki paid me kya deeper milega.Aapke hisaab se line kaha draw karni chahiye - kitna free, kitna paid?

One of the strongest growth strategies today is generosity at scale. When you consistently share real value for free, you build trust, attention, and word-of-mouth long before you ever sell anything. In a crowded market, people often buy not just the product but the credibility you earned first.

Giving value for free isn’t generosityit’s positioningIf people can learn from youthey start trusting youAnd trust compounds faster than contentThe real leverage is thisbe useful before you try to be profitable
giving value for free builds trust faster than any ad spend. people remember who taught them something useful before ever asking for anything back — that's how distribution compounds over time

This is exactly how I’ve grown on Upwork and LinkedIn.Most of my inbound came after consistently sharing real strategies and breakdowns for free.Once trust is built, clients don’t hesitate to pay. it becomes a natural next step.

This hits deeper than it looks.Most people don’t struggle with distribution…they struggle with trust.And trust isn’t built when you finally sell —it’s built in the moments where you had the chance to give… and still chose to give.That’s the paradox:The more you hold back to ‘protect value’,the more invisible you become.The ones who win long-term aren’t the smartest…they’re the most consistently generous.What’s one thing you started giving away for free that actually grew your influence?👇 Comment “VALUE” if you believe generosity compounds.
Free isn’t charity; it’s a trust acquisition strategy.The ones who win just outgive everyone else long enough to become the default.

This is literally the principle I follow as a content writer — the best briefs I've written for clients were the ones I shared publicly for free. Those free samples got me more paid work than any portfolio ever did.Give the work. The right people will find you.

This is such an important shift in perspective Raj.Because distribution isn’t built through visibility alone, it’s built through credibility earned over time.And generosity, done right, becomes one of the strongest signals of that credibility.
Trust in todays distribution is really the hardestpart. But when you believe in free distribution, how long should you give for free and what exactly should you offer?

Samay Raina did the same , he just uploaded his latent show on YouTube directly and that decision made this show very popular in a very short span of time. Distribution was the key behind his success. He was not hiding his content behind a paywell.
Trust compounds like interest Raj, the more upfront value you give, the lower the friction when monetization finally comes.

A strong insight into modern distribution. Sustainable growth comes from building trust first. Delivering consistent, high-value content without immediate expectations creates stronger relationships and long-term brand equity.
Most people try to protect value. The real game is proving it first. Give so much that people don’t question if you’re worth it—only when they can buy from you.
This is the trade most people get wrong.protecting value instead of proving it.Giving first builds trust faster than any funnel ever can.Distribution grows when generosity compounds

📌 Step by Step Guide to Build Trust Through Free Value Step 1: Identify your most valuable insights or resources. Step 2: Share them openly without expecting immediate profit. Step 3: Be consistent in delivering value over time. Step 4: Engage with your audience and listen to their needs. Step 5: Build genuine relationships based on trust, not transactions. Step 6: Introduce your paid offerings only after trust is established. Step 7: Encourage your audience to share your free content. So, when’s the last time you gave away something valuable just for the heck of it?

Your mind is not a mirror of reality—it’s a product of repetition. Feed it doubt, and it will shrink your world. Feed it belief, and it will expand your limits. Choose your inner dialogue carefully, because what you repeat daily doesn’t just reflect your life—it quietly shapes your future.
Sarita Dhar Dubey hmm... '—'

True.A year ago, I was jobless, under debt, and constantly hearing doubts from people around me.The real shift didn’t come from changing circumstances — it came from changing how I saw my situation.Once that changed, decisions became clearer, and I started building my own path.Nothing overnight, just consistent action.Interesting how things start moving once your thinking aligns.
The rise of founder-led content highlights their shift from purely business-focused to personal growth and connection with their audience.
Bhavya Gupta Feels small, but it’s not. “I’m not good enough” stayed in my head for years. It slowed everything. Tip: catch one negative line and replace it with a better one today.
Repetition shapes belief. What you keep telling yourself becomes your default lens. Change the script, and your mind starts finding proof for that instead.

commentsTranslated.28: This hits hard 💯But I have one doubt… giving free value is powerful, agreed.But is it sustainable for every creator?Because in the beginning, people give away so much for free that later monetizing feels awkward 😅Maybe the balance could be:build trust through free content, but also have clarity on what deeper value the paid offering provides.In your opinion, where should the line be drawn — how much free, how much paid?
commentsTranslated.30: Giving value for free isn't generosityit's positioningIf people can learn from youthey start trusting youAnd trust compounds faster than contentThe real leverage is thisbe useful before you try to be profitable
commentsTranslated.31: giving value for free builds trust faster than any ad spend. people remember who taught them something useful before ever asking for anything back — that's how distribution compounds over time
commentsTranslated.32: This is exactly how I've grown on Upwork and LinkedIn.Most of my inbound came after consistently sharing real strategies and breakdowns for free.Once trust is built, clients don't hesitate to pay. it becomes a natural next step.
commentsTranslated.33: This hits deeper than it looks.Most people don't struggle with distribution…they struggle with trust.And trust isn't built when you finally sell —it's built in the moments where you had the chance to give… and still chose to give.That's the paradox:The more you hold back to 'protect value',the more invisible you become.The ones who win long-term aren't the smartest…they're the most consistently generous.What's one thing you started giving away for free that actually grew your influence?👇 Comment "VALUE" if you believe generosity compounds.
commentsTranslated.34: Free isn't charity; it's a trust acquisition strategy.The ones who win just outgive everyone else long enough to become the default.
commentsTranslated.35: This is literally the principle I follow as a content writer — the best briefs I've written for clients were the ones I shared publicly for free. Those free samples got me more paid work than any portfolio ever did.Give the work. The right people will find you.
commentsTranslated.36: This is such an important shift in perspective Raj.Because distribution isn't built through visibility alone, it's built through credibility earned over time.And generosity, done right, becomes one of the strongest signals of that credibility.
commentsTranslated.41: Most people try to protect value. The real game is proving it first. Give so much that people don't question if you're worth it—only when they can buy from you.
commentsTranslated.43: 📌 Step by Step Guide to Build Trust Through Free Value Step 1: Identify your most valuable insights or resources. Step 2: Share them openly without expecting immediate profit. Step 3: Be consistent in delivering value over time. Step 4: Engage with your audience and listen to their needs. Step 5: Build genuine relationships based on trust, not transactions. Step 6: Introduce your paid offerings only after trust is established. Step 7: Encourage your audience to share your free content. So, when's the last time you gave away something valuable just for the heck of it?
commentsTranslated.44: Your mind is not a mirror of reality—it's a product of repetition. Feed it doubt, and it will shrink your world. Feed it belief, and it will expand your limits. Choose your inner dialogue carefully, because what you repeat daily doesn't just reflect your life—it quietly shapes your future.
commentsTranslated.46: True.A year ago, I was jobless, under debt, and constantly hearing doubts from people around me.The real shift didn't come from changing circumstances — it came from changing how I saw my situation.Once that changed, decisions became clearer, and I started building my own path.Nothing overnight, just consistent action.Interesting how things start moving once your thinking aligns.
commentsTranslated.48: Bhavya Gupta Feels small, but it's not. "I'm not good enough" stayed in my head for years. It slowed everything. Tip: catch one negative line and replace it with a better one today.

hook_text_english: The worst thing you can do as a leader is let someone believe they are doing great when they are not.
full_text_english: The worst thing you can do as a leader is let someone believe they are doing great when they are not. People assume that being a good leader means making people feel good about their work. The leader who softens everything, who wraps every piece of feedback in so much cushioning that the person walks out feeling good but learning nothing, everyone likes that person in the moment. But they never become the reason their team grows. The question new leaders are always asking is, "how do I say this without hurting them?" When it should actually be, "what do they actually need to hear right now?" The leaders people actually come back and thank are the ones who said the uncomfortable thing when it would have been easier not to. The ones who told you your work was not good enough while there was still time to fix it. Those conversations are never pleasant when they are happening. They only become valuable in hindsight, sometimes years later.Being honest with someone who trusts you is one of the hardest things to do because it feels like you are risking the relationship. But I have learned that you cannot grow under someone who only tells you what you want to hear. And if you are leading people, you cannot build anything real if you are choosing comfort over clarity.hashtag#rajshamani hashtag#figuringout
body_summary_english: Good leaders give honest feedback focused on what people need to hear, not on making them feel good. Leaders who soften feedback to protect feelings prevent their teams from growing.
comments: Most people don’t gatekeep because they’re strategic — they gatekeep because they’re unsure their value can stand in the open.If giving it away for free feels risky, it usually means you don’t fully trust what you’re offering yet.
Sagar Kapoor "Distribution follows generosity." This is gold. Give first. Win later.

Holding back value feels safe, but it usually slows down the very trust you’re trying to monetize later.The people who win here aren’t just generous—they’re consistent enough that the market stops questioning their depth. Raj Shamani
Swatilekha Das true. Trust is built before the sale even exists. Stop gatekeeping.

Real leverage isn’t built by chasing attention—it’s earned through consistent generosity. When you give meaningful value without expecting immediate returns, trust compounds quietly. Over time, people don’t just follow you, they believe in you—and that belief is what truly scales, far beyond any strategy or shortcut.
There’s a saying: “You can't pour from an empty cup.” Giving away value freely builds trust, and that trust fills your cup over time. It’s a slow, steady way to create something real and lasting.

This is such an important reminder. A lot of people try to protect their “best knowledge” thinking that’s what gives them an edge.  But in reality, holding back slows down trust more than it builds value. I’ve started realising that the more clearly you share what you know, the more people begin to see how you think — and that’s what actually builds credibility. Information can be copied. But clarity and consistency can’t.And that’s what eventually compounds into trust.

Nothing is wrong… and that’s exactly the problem. Comfort doesn’t look dangerous… but it quietly kills momentum. What’s one thing you know you should change but haven’t yet? 👇 hashtag#GrowthMindset hashtag#ComfortZone hashtag#SelfAwareness hashtag#Action
Free value isn’t giving away your business… it’s proving you’re worth paying for. Most people don’t lose revenue by sharing too much, they lose trust by sharing too little.
Holding back the best insights may feel safe, but sharing them openly is what actually builds long‑term influence.

Insightful perspective, sir. In today’s attention economy, trust truly compounds faster than revenue. Giving away real, actionable value not only builds credibility but also lowers the barrier for meaningful engagement. Over time, it shifts the dynamic from “selling” to “serving,” where conversions become a natural outcome of trust rather than persuasion. A great reminder that sustainable distribution is less about protecting information and more about consistently showing up with generosity and authenticity.

Raj Shamani In every industry I’ve worked in- including oil & gas- the first win may come from positioning, but the second always comes from performance. You can market a promise once. But you can only scale what consistently delivers. That’s where real businesses are built- quietly, below the surface.

Realising this early—It’s not about knowing everything before you share.It’s about sharing as you learn.People don’t connect with perfection,they connect with progress.That’s where trust actually starts.hashtag#buildinpublic hashtag#learning

Distribution today isn’t built on content alone—it’s built on trust. And trust compounds when value is given consistently, without immediate expectation of return.Holding back might protect short-term revenue, but it often limits long-term reach and influence. The ones who win are those who give enough for people to believe in them before they ever buy from them.
The hard part is patience. Most people quit before trust compounds because the ROI isn’t immediate.
Makes sense. But I wonder, how long does it usually take for giving value for free to actually turn into real distribution?

I’ve seen this play out firsthand.The people who win long-term are the ones who: → teach openly→ share transparently→ and delay monetization longer than feels comfortableBy the time they sell, the audience is already sold.Raj Shamani | Hari Baheti | Figuring Out Media & Education

I agree with this a lot. But one nuance I’ve noticed is that free value alone isn’t enough for distribution. A lot of people give away great insights but still don’t get reach because it isn’t packaged or placed where attention already exists. So it’s really a mix. Generosity builds trust, consistency builds familiarity, and distribution happens when both are combined with intentional visibility. That’s where things usually compound.

It feels counterintuitive at first, but generosity usually compounds into distribution over time.The audience builds trust long before monetization happens.What made you personally lean into this approach? Raj Shamani

Giving value for free does build trust and distribution. But not everything should be free. If you give away everything, you risk attracting people who consume but never convert.The smarter play is give enough value to earn trust, but keep depth, structure, or execution as the paid layer. Distribution isn’t just generosity it’s generosity with boundaries.

This is such a powerful mindset shift. Most people think value should be protected, but in reality, it’s generosity that builds trust and reach. When you consistently give without holding back, you don’t just grow an audience—you build belief. And once people trust you, selling isn’t convincing… it’s a natural next step. That’s how real, lasting distribution is created. ✨

People don’t follow value—they follow consistency of value. Giving away your best ideas isn’t losing leverage, it’s building trust at scale. By the time you sell, you’re not convincing, you’re converting. Distribution isn’t about reach first, it’s about trust compounding over time.

Gatekeeping feels safe. Generosity feels like risk. But only one of them scales.  We confuse “value” with “inventory.” You don’t run out of insights by sharing them. You run out of trust by hoarding them.  The irony: the people most scared to give away their “best stuff” don’t realize it only becomes their best stuff _after_ the market validates it. And the market can’t validate what it never sees.  “Distribution is just an unreasonable amount of value, given freely” — that line should be on every founder’s wall. Because attention isn’t bought anymore. It’s earned in installments of free, and the interest is trust.  Well said, Raj. The paywall goes up easy. The moat gets built when people would riot if you stopped posting for free.

Trust builds when value is shared consistently and openly. Offering it freely doesn’t lower your worth, it makes it easier for people to believe in you. When results are visible, the decision to pay feels obvious, not pressured. The real test isn’t tactics, it’s staying patient.

most people confuse protecting value with building leverage, but in distribution, hiding your best work usually slows trust more than it protects revenue.the real compounding happens when people experience your best thinking before they ever have to pay for it.

This flips the usual instinct and rightly so Raj.Most people protect value to monetise it.The few who win distribute value to compound trust.Because in today’s market, attention isn’t scarce, trust is.When you consistently give away insight that actually helps:→ You reduce perceived risk→ You build authority without saying it→ You let results speak before you sellAnd that changes the buying dynamic completely.By the time you monetise, you’re not convincing, you’re converting belief into action.The real trade-off isn’t free vs paid.It’s short-term extraction vs long-term leverage.Give enough value, long enough, and distribution stops being a strategy.It becomes reputation.

Giving away value for free isn't a new concept; it’s ancient wisdom. In India, profound teachings like the Gita have always been available to everyone for free. Yet, the lesson remains: accessibility doesn't guarantee transformation. Many speak about the Gita, but only a few actually apply its value. Free distribution builds the bridge, but the audience still has to do the work to cross it
what worked for us was giving away the “what” for free but keeping the “how to implement step-by-step” paid. people don’t pay for info anymore, they pay for clarity and execution

One of the strongest growth strategies today is generosity at scale. When you consistently share real value for free, you build trust, attention, and word-of-mouth long before you ever sell anything. In a crowded market, people often buy not just the product but the credibility you earned first.
